TERMREASON: Bad conditions (deep snow, avalanching, falling ice, or rock)
TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6300m due to deep snow and old tangled ropes
ROUTEMEMO:
The route normally is mainly rock climbing up to C2, but because of the "excessive amount of snow, it was a completely different climb with deep snow from BC....We found during our stay in the Khumbu the weather to be completely unpredictable this season."

"We left C2 at 3:00 am on the 5th of May and took six hours to climb the Grey Tower, a climb that should have only taken 2.5 hours. Deep snow and a tangle of old fixed ropes made it difficult to climb safely. We reached the top of the Mushroom Ridge (6300m) at 9:00 am and decided the conditions were going to make it difficult to get to the summit and back safely, so we turned around. Later that morning the weather changed for the worse, and it seemed like we had made the right decision."

The top of the ridge was the team's high point. It was reached by Groom, Morgans, Robson, Sharp and Tucker, plus Lal Singh and Furba Tamang. That same day, 5th of May, they returned to C1, on the 6th they removed all their equipment to BC, and on the 8th they left BC.

Farmer never reached BC. He developed cerebral edema during the approach march and left the team by helicopter from Pheriche. Oxygen was used by Farmer at Pheriche Hospital.

SPONSOR: Seven Summit Treks Annapurna I Expedition 2015
APPROACH: Pokhara, from Tatopani to BC by helicopter
ROUTEMEMO:

C1  05/03  5100m

           Mingma Gyalje, Nima Ongchu, Nima Gyalzen, Kaji, Angeli, Pemba 
           between 3:15 pm and 4 pm.

Team left C4 at 12:30 am. Weather was clear and calm. The first 300m of the climb had fixed rope; however, afterwards the team split in groups of 6 and 7 and moved on a main rope together. The ascent was straightforward and the group reached the summit between 3:15 pm and 4 pm. They stayed for about 15 minutes to take pictures.

On the descent they used three main ropes to abseil down, which with 13 people took quite some time. When they reached 7400m at about 11:30 pm on 24 March, they were all very tired and decided to wait for the sun to come up. Mansikka and Pemba Sherpa continued their descent, however, without a rope. Shahlaee saw the headlamps from his spot at 7400m, but all of a sudden at about 3 am they were gone. The assumption is that Pemba Sherpa took a fall, bumped into Mansikka and they both fell to their deaths. On 26 March a helicopter came to look for the bodies and could identify them an altitude of about 7100m. It was impossible to retrieve them.

The 11 team members and Sherpas, who rested at 7400m waited for the sun to rise and continued down at about 6 am. After many abseils, they reached C4 at 10 am. Everyone stayed C4 and continued on 26 March. Apart from Irmak and Ngima, who stayed at C2, everyone descended to BC where they arrived at 8 pm on 26 March. Irmak and Ngima reached BC on 27 March.

The Spanish doctor Carlos visited their camp and helped them for frostbite and bandaged them. On 28 March a helicopter came and picked up the team.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 5200m due to earthquake
ROUTEMEMO:
Despite the fact that on 25 April there had been an earthquake that was disastrous for teams on the Nepalese side of Everest, this team was not aware of any serious problems on the Tibet side of Cho Oyu. They had arrived at Chinese BC for Cho Oyu on 22 April, and on the 27th all members except Merritt trekked about 300 meters up a hill from BC and returned to BC that day.

The 28th was a rest day, and they learned then that the Chinese authorities had cancelled all climbs in Tibet because of the earthquake. So on the 30th they left BC and went by road to Tingri and on to Lhasa. They spent two nights in Lhasa and on 3 May flew back to Kathmandu.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 5200m due to earthquake
ROUTEMEMO:
BC  20/04  4850m
ABC 24/04  5650m	
HPt 24/04  5650m by Biswas.

When the earthquake hit at about 11:50 am Nepali time, Biswas was in the dining tent, writing his dairy. It was a severe vibration and lasted for about a minute. He instantly came out and called the other members as they were all inside their tents. There was a strong wind at ABC and it took them a while to realize that this was an earthquake. Biswas was scared about the serac that was towering above their tent, but luckily nothing happened. He could then hear the sound of avalanches from the eastern slopes, which were far away.

They had to abandon the climb due to the Chinese Authority's decision to stop mountaineering for the season in Tibet.

The BC doctor, Marisa Eve Girawong, a physician's assistant specializing in emergency medicine, was killed by the earthquake-triggered avalanche in BC on 25 April.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6400m due to earthquake
ROUTEMEMO:
No members had ventured far above BC before the earthquake struck the area on 25 April and triggered avalanches. But on 23 April all Everest (and Lhotse) members, plus 9 Sherpas, did go to just below C1 (to 5900m) and returned to BC the same day.

The team had a C1 at 6000m, but no one slept in it. All 14 of the expedition's climbing Sherpas carried loads to C2 (6400m) on the 20th and 24th and trekked back to BC. The team's two cooks/kitchen boys, Pemba Ngatar Sherpa and Pasang Lakpa Sherpa, also went up to C2 on the 20th and stayed there. During the earthquake, many crevasses opened up between C1 and C2 making it necessary to evacuate the kitchen staff by helicopter; they flew out to BC on the 27th.

On the day after it was decided to abandon the climb, and the members left BC on 1 May. They trekked to Pheriche, from where they flew by helicopter to Lukla. They went from there by plane to Biratnagar and thence to Kathmandu. The leader left BC on 3 May: he found a seat in a helicopter going directly to Kathmandu.

The team's only casually was Barbara Padilla, who suffered some broken teeth when she fell while running to a tent to take cover from an advancing avalanche on 25 April.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6400m due to earthquake
ROUTEMEMO:
The expedition occupied BC at 5350m on 12 April, and all the members established C1 at 6100m on 24 April. They were forced to stay there by the disaster of the earthquake on 25 April that caused devastating avalanching from Pumori. Seven members were evacuated from C1 to BC by helicopter on 26 April and the rest of the members the next day.

Some of the load-carrying Sherpas went up to C2 at 6400m and slept there, but none of the members moved above C1.

Six of their Sherpas were killed by the avalanches: five on the day of the earthquake, 25 April, and one died in Kathmandu on the 30th from his injuries. They were:

  Chhimi Dawa Sherpa, 26/8/67, Bung-9, Solu
  Dawa Chhiri Sherpa, 25/11/89, Chaurikharka, Solu
  Jangbu Sherpa, cook, 9/5/61, Taksindu-5, Solu (died in Kathmandu)
  Milan Rai, kitchen staff, 9/5/73, Bung-4, Solu
  Pemba Sherpa, cook, 25/2/96, Taksindu-5, Solu
  Pemba Yish (Hissi) Sherpa, 31/1/82, Bung-9, Solu

Most of the team's members left BC by helicopter on 30th April; the leader and three members walked to Lukla and flew in a plane from there to Kathmandu.

Two Sherpas were seriously injured by the quake; they and 7 others were evacuated by helicopter.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6100m due to earthquake
ROUTEMEMO:
The team established BC on 12 April, and C1 was occupied on the 24th by all 4 Everest members and their only Lhotse climber in their brief acclimatization climb.  They descended to BC in the morning of the 25th, a few hours before the earthquake struck.

Fortunately they had pitched their BC close to the foot of the Khumbu Icefall, so it was not damaged by the avalanching triggered by the earthquake that destroyed camps farther away from the Icefall. All their tents were undamaged except for the shower tent, which had been tied down and therefore ripped.

The team stayed in BC until 1 May and then trekked to Lukla and flew to Kathmandu on the 4th.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6500m due to avalanche triggered by earthquake
ROUTEMEMO:
All members occupied C1 (6100m) on 23 April and next day climbed up to the site of C2 (6400m), but returned to C1 for the night. On the 25th the earthquake triggered avalanching off Pumori at 11:55 am. The team had descended to BC at 11:40 am.

They abandoned their attempt to climb Everest, but they spent three nights in their BC, which was at the top end of the base camps and was undamaged. They helped during these next days in the efforts to rescue climbers who were trapped in and above BC.

On the 27th they trekked to Pheriche and from there flew by helicopter to Lukla.  On 1 May they reached Kathmandu by plane.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6400m due to earthquake in Nepal
ROUTEMEMO:
All 12 members moved into ABC at 6400m on 24 April and were there when the earthquake caused the earth to shake the next day at midday. Fortunately, the north side of the mountain suffered from no avalanching or any other misfortune. The team descended to Chinese BC (5200m) on the 26th.

On the 28th, the China Tibet Mountaineering Association informed Everest climbers that all climbing within Tibet was officially "terminated" during the 2015 spring season because of the earthquake; the written version of this announcement was dated 1 May. On 3 May, yaks brought the team's equipment downt to BC; on the 4th they were driven to Shigatse and on the 5th to Lhasa. They flew to Kathmandu on the 8th.

From SummitClimb website:

The season began in early April, after a snowy winter. The snow continued to fall lightly through the middle of April and the temperatures remained moderate. Our teams began arriving in Tibet on 9 April. An initial acclimatization cycle brought the team up to 6400 metre / 21,000 foot high Advanced Base Camp, from which an attempt to reach the 7000 metre / 23,000 foot high North Col (camp 1) was launched. On 25 April, a 7.5 earthquake hit the region. Although very few, if any, effects of the earthquake were felt on the Tibet side of Everest, the China Tibet Mountaineering Association chose to end the climbing season and bring everone home, due to casualties and infrastructure effects elsewhere in Tibet and also in Nepal, where many of the mountain staff are from.

SPONSOR: Ranveer Singh Jamwal Indian Army Everest-Lhotse Massif Exped 2015
TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6400m due to earthquake
ROUTEMEMO:
The Indian army mounted a large effort to scale Everest and Lhotse with 22 climbing members (16 Indian citizens and 6 Nepalese) from various army regiments, plus eight men from the Army Adventure Wing to assist in administration. Morever, two of the climbers, Ranveer Singh Jamwal and Hajari Lal, planned to summit Everest, descend to the South Col and summit Lhotse.

But despite the meticulous planning and climbers high hopes, it all came to naught when on 25 April a serious earthquake triggered fatal avalanching off of Pumori and Nuptse that devastated the Khumbu Valley. After-shocks continued for some time, and when this team left BC on 4 May, Pumori was still avalanching. A large part of Everest's base camp area was obliterated, but this team's camp was safe.

10 Sherpas had carried loads to C2 and marked out this team's camp area on 23 April. They were safely back in BC on the 25th. Jamwal, the leader, told his members on 27-28 April that he did not think it would be possible to do any more climbing this season, and when it was clear that no one would be refixing the route up to C2, he formally decided to abandon the climb on 4 May.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 5350m due to earthquake
ROUTEMEMO: The team was one of the last to go to BC. Their plan was to attempt Lobuche East to acclimatize and used Lobuche Lodge as their base for this climb without Sherpa help. But they arrived there on the day of the earthquake, 25 April, so they did no climbing on Lobuche East or Everest. On the 27th they walked back to Everest BC to pack up all their belongings at a BC that had been devastated, and went on to Pangboche. On 1 May they flew by helicopter to Lukla, flew by plane to Biratnagar, and from there to Kathmandu on the same day.

High point team was at C2 during the earthquake. David Liano describes it as follows: "It was overcast, but following the first ground movement, a massive avalanche broke off Nuptse. Then the ground really started shaking and we could see, but mostly hear, avalanches being released from Nuptse, Lhotse, Everest (lots of rockfall from the west face) and the West Shoulder. Many seracs collapsed from the West Shoulder. The team was picked up by helicopter in between C1 and C2 and flown back to BC on 27 April. Nobody got hurt."

Rashid, Kumar and Mangar were at C1 when the avalanche happened. They were picked up by helicopter from C1 on April 27th and flown back to BC.

Loveraj was at BC during the avalanche as well as the Leader/Doctor/Base Camp Manager, Dr. Nima Sherpa, Carsten Lillelund Pedersen (Denmark) and Jelle Veyt (Belgium) and seven members of the kitchen staff. The whole base camp of Asian Trekking survived intact.

Wojciech by email:

We were in the mess tent in BC (Seven Summit Treks) in the highest camp in whole BC when the earthquake happened. After 3-4 seconds we heard the sound of avalanches wall about 30-50m high rolling down in our direction from the lower part of BC. It was in 200m distance when whe saw it. We ran 10-15m and hid behind the big rock near the mess tent. After 2 minutes we left our hideout, a little bit covered with snow powder. No one from our agency got destroyed, also no injured. The rest of the day after earthquake we worked hard helping injured people from the destroyed part of BC, carried injured on self-made stretchers about 1 km down to medical tents in IMG agency. Unfortunately some of them died in the night, because first helicopters came next day after the tragedy (bad weather and no visibility).

They stayed about 30 minutes on the summit and descended to C3, where Bem picked up his skis and skied down all the way to BC. He reached it at about 8 pm.

He did not think it was too bad; however, when he got to Samagaon the pain was too much and he decided to take a helicopter back to Kathmandu on 20 April. We considered trying to arrange to get more supplies and more oxygen, but after the scale of the earthquake devastation in other parts of Nepal became evident and learning it would not be possible to fly in new supplies (as helicopters were naturally required elsewhere) we had to abandon any plans to return for another summit push.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6500m due to earthquake and avalanche
ROUTEMEMO:
Hahn's team were one of the earliest to get to BC and therefore were able to pitch their camp not far below the Khumbu Icefall, when the the earthquake triggered avalanching suddenly descended on the BC area on 25 April, their BC was in "a good spot and just got dusted by the enormous slides off of Pumori." No one on this expedition was killed or seriously injured. It was the middle third of the mile-long area of various camps that was devastated by the quake.

At the time of the quake, all RMI members except Seaton, who had various health problems, and all climbing Sherpas were in C1 at 6000m. They had reached C1 on the 23rd, and during the period 24-26 April the seven members and 11 Sherpas who were in C1 rotated higher and "touched" C2 at 6500m. But the day after the quake it was clear that it was "hopeless" to try climb Everest, and the decision to abandon the expedition was taken on the 28th.

The people in C2 spent a total of four nights there, then flew by helicopter to BC and left on the 30th. By this time no one was going to attempt the mountain: the Icefall doctors, who install the fix ropes all the way to C2, had fled. No member had set food above C2; there were no fixed lines above there, and the whole area was still shaking days after the quake.

Hahn is not keen to climb Everest again. He has summited it 15 times and made five other attempts. It is much too crowded nowadays; he would enjoy climbing alone, but not in the massive crowds that Everest attracts.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6100m due to earthquake

ROUTEMEMO:
The Himex Everest and Lhotse expeditions were early arrivals at BC: their camp was established on 10 April. The members went next to Lobuche East to acclimatize, and all summited it on 14 April. Then they returned to Everest BC on the 18th.

The next day one Everest member, Julian Mountain, ventured into the Icefall as far as the "football field" at about 5500m. Meanwhile their Sherpas moved up to C1 to dump loads and descend to BC from the 12th to the 19th.

But there was no significant movement of these members or Sherpas on Everest or Lhotse any higher. The earthquake hit the area of the 25th of April. The Icefall doctors, the Sherpas who install the fixed ropes up to C2, fled and climbing came to an abrupt halt.

The Himex climbers were evacuated from C1 and BC on 6 May in an orientation that began with the Himex Makalu team's evacuation from Hillary BC by helicopters that then picked up the Khumbu teams and flew all of them to Kathmandu.

Team was on their acclimatization trip when they reached their high point on 24 April. During the earthquake they were in their dining tent at BC and got badly shaken and dusted by the avalanche.

They decided to leave BC midday on 26 April as soon as their Sherpas were safely back from C2. They relocated to Pheriche to wait there to assess the situation for a few days. They then decided on 29th April to keep descending as it was clear that the season could not restart.

The team remained in Kathmandu until 15 May doing relief work.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6500m due to earthquake
ROUTEMEMO:
First to arrive at BC were Francois from Belgium and the climbing Sherpas, who reached BC on 4 April, in good time to get a head start and to pitch their camp where they wanted it, not far from the Icefall. But they had to wait for their baggage to turn up: it was delayed by bad weather. The four Singaporeans reached BC on the 9th of April and the Malaysians [on same team/permit] came the following day.

Before tackling Everest, four Singaporeans, their Belgian teammate, and five Sherpas including the team's leader Jamling climbed to the summit of Lobuche East on 13 April. Then on the 14th, the whole team ventured into the Khumbu Icefall. The Four Singaporeans and the Belgian got as far as 100 meters below C1, which was at the top of the Icefall at about 6000m. Two Malaysians [Ishak and Mahamad Yusoff], climbing very slowly, reached 200 meters lower than the Singaporeans (see EVER-151-44). All slept in BC that night. Meanwhile nine load-carrying Sherpas had dumped loads as high as C2 (6500m) on the previous day.

For more acclimatization, on 18th the team climbed on the lower reaches of Pumori, and three days later they were on Kalapattar, then returned to BC that day. Now they had to wait at BC on the 22nd and 23rd because of snowfall. They spent the 24th packing for their ascent to C1 next day.

They never got there: a devastating earthquake struck the area on the 25th. This team's BC camp was close enough to the bottom of the Icefall to be spared fatal damage by the tremor, but the camps lower than it was gravely hit. Jamling's teams waited for the shaking to subside and to learn what would happen next. On 30 April they decided there would be no more climbing; they cancelled their plan to attempt Everest. Most of the members left BC the next day by helicopter to Lukla and fixed wing to Kathmandu. Jamling flew by helicopter to Kathmandu on the 5th.

All their tents were undamaged except for the shower tent, which had been tied down and therefore ripped.

From Dipankar Ghosh blog:
 http://dipankarghosh.info/expedition-stories/410775/mt-annapurna-i-expedition-2015

Mt. Annapurna-I Expedition, 2015

Run, run, run. Suddenly, Kim and Zeen took to their heels. They jumped over the rocks, dodged the trees - synergizing the hooting sound they expelled from their lungs while they climbed up and whooshed down the uneven trail. We laughed heartily as a village boy looked at the running duo with gaping mouth and purest form of surprise written all over his face. We had settled at the outskirt of a village called Swata having decided to have our lunch in picnic style. All of us were busy peeling potatoes as it was only mashed potato in the menu and Kim and Zeen were running and chasing each other perhaps to increase their appetite. 

Frankly speaking, I was enjoying this five day tension free, hassle free trek to Tatopani. Our path meandered through the dense forest of Pine and Christmas trees bordered by rhododendron and magnolia which bloomed in full thrust at this time of the year. Last year, during the Kanchenjunga expedition I had met this Korean pair Kim and Yum. Since then we had developed strong bond of likings towards each other. This year, we decided to embark on a joint expedition.
 
After Everest, Lhotse, Makalu and Kanchenjunga, this time I went to Nepal eyeing the World’s 10th highest peak, Mount Annapurna – I (8091m). Ours was an Indo-Korean team that comprised of four climbers from Korea. I was the only one representing India. On 19th of March, 2015 the members of the two countries gathered at Thamel. Here we did our shopping and completed our packing. We hired a small car and travelled across Pokhra to reach Nayapull. 

From Nayapull we boarded another car which dropped us at the border of Ghandruk from where an hour’s uphill march took us to Ghandruk. Mountain lovers adore this village. From here the base camp of Annapurna south could be reached along a trail. However, our destination was Tatopani. Annapurna south stood majestically before us. 

The path from Lete that would take us to the base camp was laden with snow and the local people were reluctant to wet their feet. I couldn’t blame them. Anyway, our agency failed to arrange for low altitude porters and decided to shift our luggage by helicopter - the only alternative left. 

However, reaching Annapurna north base camp from Tatopani meant an ascent of 4200m at one go and we were apprehensive that it might cause high altitude sickness. Therefore, we decided to trek to Tatopani. As we slowly marched uphill crunching snow under our feet nobody regretted the five day long journey. When we reached Bailley we had a magnificent view of peak Dhaulagiri. On 26th we reached Tatopani beside Kaligondoki River. Sanu our Sherpa Sardar and his team also arrived with a truck load of stores. Excluding our personal rucksacks the load weighed 1980 kg. The helicopter had to make five trips to shift the entire load. 

On 28th, at the foot of Mt. Annapurna-I and on a huge ground our base camp was established. We had a hard time sifting the huge volume of load. The base camp was located at the north side of Mt. Annapurna-I and we had a splendid view of the peak from here. 

Almost one month prior to us three teams were labouring on the peak. During summit approach of one of these teams - three days back - Samuli of Finland and a Sherpa had lost their lives. During Makalu and Kanchenjunga expedition I had spent some good time with Samuli. He was a good climber. Thrice a helicopter was sent to recover their bodies. At 7200m their bodies could be traced but due to difficult terrain their bodies could not be recovered. I was really sad. 

On the 31st amidst bad weather the base camp puja was performed. Later, we had snowfall. One day’s snowfall changed the entire feature of the base camp. The cold was bitter. To beat the cold Kim had had an ingenious idea. They had brought huge tents from their homeland. We put our dome tents inside one such big tent. Amazingly, it gave us all the warmth of a luxurious hotel suite. 

On 1st of April, we set out for camp-I to ferry load. After a march half an hour we reached a place where the climbers used to establish their base camp in the past. But, few years back a devastating avalanche caused few deaths and since then the place had been declared unsuitable as a campsite. We noticed a cenotaph built in the memory of those unfortunate climbers. We prayed for their souls to the Almighty and moved on. 

To reach camp-I one had to descend on the Annapurna north glacier. Then one had to cross the ice field and ascend along a rock buttress. Here and there, avalanches are frequent. We climbed few steep gradients. We were lucky to discover old ropes which certainly added to our advantage. On reaching the top of the rock buttress we further climbed an ice slope to a height of 5060m. We had to scoop up and clear snow to pitch our tents. That day we descended to base camp. 

While descending, we thought that we were doomed. There were avalanches on both sides of our path and we had to move very watchfully. In the evening, we had strong winds at the base camp. It was very bad weather, indeed. 

I was getting used to eating all types of food in the company of the Koreans. Larva of Butterfly, Kimchi made up our daily menu. Even dried octopus found place in our snacks! After resting one day at the base camp, on 3rd we went up to Camp-I. From Camp-I we had a magnificent view of Mt. Annapurna-I. Though the weather was bad after the noon - it improved in the evening. However, we had strong wind velocity that untiringly swept the camp, moaning and wailing and making our life miserable. 

On 4th of April, 2015, at the break of the dawn we set off for Camp-II. The climb was interesting. We had to cross a ice field. We climbed few steep gradients. The distance between Camp-I and Camp-II was comparatively small. We dumped our load on a forty degree slope at an altitude of 5600 meter and amidst bad weather returned to the base camp. 

Very next day, a dreadful avalanche washed away our tents from camp-I which were packed with climbing boots, crampons, sleeping bags, down jackets and many more essential equipment. Sanu and his team returned. They had traced few equipment but could not recover any. Next day we carried on with the search operation. From faraway places and risky zones we could recover many equipment but still many could not be found. However, the members of other teams extended helping hand and the loss of our equipments could be complemented for. We were really grateful to them. 

Sometimes, we played golf. I don’t know whether we set new records of playing golf at high altitudes but we enjoyed the sport niftily tailored to suit our needs. The weather was not conducive–either snow fell or wind blew strongly. This year the weather was not favoring us. 

On 10th we went up to Camp-II. At 5600m, we pitched our tents on an ice slope where we dumped our loads. 

Generally for better acclimatization, the climbers spend one night at Camp-II and return. A stretch between Camp-II and Camp-III was avalanche prone and generally every climber wanted to cross it only once. However, this was Lee and Zeen’s first expedition. That is why Kim thought it was necessary for them to do some initial altitude gain.

Consequently, on the following day they accompanied Sanu while ferrying load to Camp-III. It was a windy day. The route to Camp-III was quite long. At 6500m height Sanu (our Sherpa sardar) and other sherpas had dumped our load. On the same day we returned to base camp. The process of acclimatization ended. 

Now, we rested, feed ourselves and waited for favourable weather window to open. After eleven days, on 22nd, our sherpas went up to Camp-III to ferry loads for one more time. On reaching Camp-II they found that our tents had crushed under the snow. Enroute they fell in front of an avalanche. They escaped with minor injuries. 
On 23rd, weather was not so good; few climbers of another team and their Sherpas launched a summit attempt. We spent anxious moments at the base Camp for them. On 24th, one Spanish member of that team fell seriously ill. A helicopter was sent for his rescue. After two unsuccessful attempts, the pilot could rescue him. 

On 25th, a violent tremor shook the mountains. We were inside our tents when the earthquake began. It was avalanches everywhere. We dashed out of our tents and ran straight across the field on the opposite end. There, we perched ourselves on a mound and waited for the quake to subside. There was a steep slope overlooking our tents and we feared avalanches would roll down from there any moment and crush our tents. It was our good fortune that nothing of the sort happened. 

Next day, we again felt a jolt. Whether it was an aftershock or not, it was quite strong. Later we learned that the quake on the second day measured 6.6 in the Richter scale whereas the first one had been recorded at 7.9. 

Reports of huge damage and deaths in Nepal began to flood in. I was thinking if we could stand beside the people of Nepal in these bad hours. Our training and our gears may come handy in the relief work. I had been trained for search and rescue course from NIM(India) and I thought it was one chance where my training could be of some use. Above all, mountaineer or no mountaineer it is one’s duty to rush to the help of poor suffering people. Kim too was thinking on the same lines. We deliberated and decided in the team meeting that we would go down as fast as possible–and try to be of some help to the quake affected people of Nepal. It was more important to hold their hands than touching the summit of Mt. Annapurna. We learned that American team leaded by Don too had decided to descend. 

We started descending. We descended four days route in one day and rushed to the nearest village. It was a horrific picture of destructions everywhere. Buildings collapsed, people houseless –the merciless paws of nature had spread mayhem. We tried our best to help the villagers. They were in miserable state – their belongings trapped under the debris. We tried to make them temporary shelters and recover few of their properties that were trapped under the wreckage. 

It was an altogether different experience for all of us. We were not sorry that we had allowed the peak to go from our hand. We had opened the route to camp-III. Rather, it gave us more joy to think that we could stand beside the people of Nepal when they required it most. We were happy to touch their hearts and earn their blessings. With a belief that I would certainly get a chance to get back to the peak if the Providence has it in store for me. 

I returned to my homeland on 12th May.

Tranlated by Google from Norwegian blog:
  http://www.utemagasinet.no/Aktuelt/Vi-foeler-oss-heldige

- We feel lucky
- We celebrate life. We are on the rim of an extreme disaster, we feel wonderful lucky, says Inge Meloy. Utemagasinet did speak with the manager of Hvitserk Everest Expedition Tuesday afternoon.

By: Erlend Sande Erlend Sande Wednesday 29 April 2015 kl.09: 56S

Along with five Norwegian climbers and five of the expedition klatresherpaer, was Inge Meloy (39) caught at 6400 meters altitude when the earthquake in Nepal triggered a number of large avalanches on Mount Everest Saturday. Ruta down was swept away by landslides, and they did not know whether members of the expedition down the base camp had survived. When utemagasinet.no spoke with expedition leader yesterday afternoon, they were in the village of Namche Bazaar at 3400 meters altitude, after three intense days.

- Now we have gone down, we have passed the dead people and damaged buildings. They sing, it perches, Nepali people bury their dead long the route we go. We are part of a major disaster, we wish there was more we could do. Our doctor has worked hard for those who were in the base camp. We provide all the assistance we can, which is less than what we wish, says the father of two, police officer and expedition leader.

On Sunday Hvitserk Expedition brought down to base camp and a sight that made tremendous impression.

- Our camp was in the middle of the base camp, which is one kilometer long. The part where we were completely gone. Erased, he says.

What would you have done if you were in the camp when exceeded arrived?

- We'd be somewhere and read, slumbered in tents. All our seven tents were gone. The equipment lay everywhere. I found Mac my 200 meters away from the camp. Had we been there ... because I dare not think of what had happened.

In the base camp at 5300 meters was expedition physician Erling Rose Power. When he saw the earthquake, he realized that there was a risk of avalanches and managed to get in the top five of the expedition's Sherpas who had rest day in camp. All arrived in safety in a dump, but the one Sherpas lost three front teeth when he was hit by ice and rock. 20 people died in the landslide, but 73 injured were flown to hospital.

- We are 19 undamaged less three incisors, says Meloy, who adds that the Norwegian expedition members have decided to splice the dental bill.

Utemagasinet talks with Meloy on an unstable telephone line. On Wednesday morning he updated his Facebook profile for the first time after the earthquake. "Today, we celebrate life!", He concludes there. But still, the five Norwegian climbers, doctor Erling Rose Power and Meloy in a very unstable and unclear situation in the mountains in the disaster-hit Nepal. They live out on a football field and do not know when and how they are getting home. It is difficult to obtain food.

"It breeds every day. We can not lower your shoulders. "

Inge Meloy, head of Hvitserk Everest Expedition 2015

- There are aftershocks every day. It goes soil and rock slides. It's hard to get something to eat. Everything is closed, no one dares to stay inside and cook. There are rocks as big as houses that scroll across the trail. But we're not in constant danger. Much is safe, but there is uncertainty around how we get home. It breeds every day. We can not lower your shoulders. As police, I'm used to thinking "situation awareness". You must be disciplined, lower your shoulders at the right time, take the assessment continuously.

Are you afraid?

- It is uncomfortable. Earthquake-theoretically, we know that the biggest quake is over, but we does not lower your shoulders. Built in Namche Bazaar, nobody wants to be. The stones can detach from the mountainside. Every day and every night there are aftershocks. It's uncomfortable.

He is interrupted by sounds in the background.

- Now call bells, Nepali people bury their dead. We visit here. We stand in the middle of a disaster and feel lucky because we have survived, repeat Meloy.
When an earthquake measured at 7.9 on the Richter scale struck mountainous Nepal on Saturday, the Norwegian Everest Expedition on the rise in the mountains.

- We were about to get up to Camp 2 at 6500 meters when the earthquake struck. We stood on a moraine ridge and saw that it came the avalanche from all sides. I figured at first did not know what happened. It was like "Wow, that was close, oj, here there is plenty." The terrain is like a horseshoe with Everest, Lhotse and Nuptse and there were landslides in all mountain sides. It slammed and shook. Some shouted "run" and I thought 'where should I run?". There are not many steps you're able to run with 15 kilograms on his back in 6500 meters altitude. So it was an inferno of snow and air, says Meloy.

Then it was quiet.

- Fortunately it happened nothing more, nothing hit us. There is luck, there is no calculation, where, he admits.

"There were four to five hours where I did not know what happened to our boys. Then I had a stomach ache."

What did you do next?

- We were in a vulnerable position. There were large chunks of ice above us. So we conc to get us into the camp. Our four tents were behind a moraine mound. I called Hvitserk and said that there had been an earthquake and landslide, but that we were in good condition.

When you realized that it was an earthquake?

- We realized quite quickly. Sirdaren (sjefssherpaen, ed.'s Note.) Our said it instantly when we went to its knees, we were unable to keep our legs. I had recently been to a dinner with some British geologists who said that a major earthquake in Nepal was 50 years overdue, so I thought about what I had heard.
What did you know about the situation in the base camp?

- There were four to five hours where I did not know what happened to our boys. Then I had a stomach ache. It's not fun to be the head and not know how guys are. After a few hours we were notified that there was going well with our people. Doctor, Erling, had lost everything in the landslide, but had borrowed by satellite phone and called home. I had to have the first insider information, otherwise there are rumors. It was hard the first four to five hours.

What were you thinking about the future?

- We knew we had limited food and resources. Then it is well with talented people in the back office, Lars Oma Erichsrud have been through the icefall and know how it is there. He knew that there would not be navigable for a long time. Hvitserk arranged simply transport, so we were the first to be flown out, he says.
All the staff at Hvitserk office was in Nepal earlier spring. To prepare for the expedition, they had meetings with the embassy, hospitals, rescue services and helicopter companies. The goal was to have the best support system, even though the Norwegian tour operator had not had people on top of the world's highest mountain earlier.

Meloy knew it was out of the question to try to go down to base camp. That would mean crossing the Khumbu glacier falls. The route is secured with an intricate system of ladders and fixed ropes, which apparently was swept away by landslides. 107 individuals were caught at 6500 meters altitude. Meloy, who has led over 100 rescues in the job with the Governor, took the lead.

"There were two in Camp 2 which was critically ill, one sherpa and a western. They were flown out as the first. They saved probably life on it."

Inge Meloy, head of Hvitserk Everest expedition

- I decided that the route to Camp 1 was not open. I stopped a British climber, who thanked me afterwards for having saved his life, he says.

When two helicopters came in the air Sunday, the first used to evacuate injured from base camp. So they were available to transport the Norwegian expedition down.
Meloy emphasizes that they were extremely careful with their evacuation should not take helicopter capacity that could have been used to transport the injured to hospital. Another assumption was that sherpas and western climbers should be treated equally.

- There were two in Camp 2 which was critically ill, one Sherpa and a westerner. They were flown out as the first. They saved probably life on it. Then there was us. We were flown out on Sunday, thanks to a good plan. I took the role of manager effort. Patients were out first, then us.

Was there some conflict about who did fly first?

- There was no conflict, except that the sherpas did not quite understand that they should also be included. We were flown down with eight boost to base camp after all patients in the base camp were rescued from there.

One helicopter could take one passenger, the other could take two. Each lift has a price tag of $10,000.

- It is extremely to be fetched at 6400 meters. The day after they brought the 100 others. Thanks backofficen to Hvitserk we were at first, repeat Meloy.

It turned out that evacuation by helicopter was only safe way to get down to those of Khumbu glacier when earthquake. More are missing after trying to take off down the mountain on foot.

"There have not been many statements about the disappointment the last two days. People have invested a year and a half million, but is primarily pleased that the team is together. I marvel and rejoice in it.

Inge Meloy, expedition leader on Everest in 2015

It is the first time a Norwegian tour operator has Everest on the program. Inge Meloy leads a group in which each participant has paid 500,000 to experience the dream of climbing the world's highest mountain.

- It's the most joy of living, but also disappointment over not to have got tried out. Everyone can go home to your family, it's fundamental feel. I think there's room for two things in mind at once. But there have been many statements about the disappointment the last two days. People have invested a year and a half million, but is primarily pleased that the team is together. I marvel and rejoice in it, he says.

From Alpine Ascents Wordpress Blog:

ALPINE ASCENTS TEAMS AT CAMP I AND BASE CAMP STAFF (April 25, 2015)

I was able to speak to Lakpa Rita at base camp and along with other teams we are assisting with those effected by the avalanche at base camp. Those located in the center of the sprawling tent camp were most effected. Our larger tents are being used a medical tents and will report should there be any further development. All our climbers and Sherpa staff are OK.

ALPINE ASCENTS TEAM UPDATE FROM CAMP I (April 26, 2015)

I spoke with guide Eric Murphy early this morning and team is well at Camp I. Alpine Ascents guide Damian Benegas along with other climbers and guides tried to establish a route to base camp through the ice fall, but tremors made this impossible. At this time; the teams at base camp feel a route through the ice fall will be very difficult and thus we are looking to use helicopter transport to move our and other teams from Camp I to base camp. Though a few climbers have already been transported,  Eric informed us that the heli transport on a large scale should begin tomorrow. Non Alpine Ascents teams that are at Camp II will be moving to Camp I and all outfitters such as International Mountain Guides, Rainier Mountaineering, Madison Mountaineering, Adventure Consultants and other brethren outfitters are working together both at Base Camp and Camp I. We have heard of life lost at base camp and of course we all share this loss with our fellow climbing teams – offering support and condolences as we can – to the climbing community and the larger community in Nepal.

BACK IN BASE CAMP (April 27, 2015)

We just received word that climbers are back in Base Camp. We have a few staff members/Sherpas that were at Camp II and should have arrived at base camp as well or to be flown shortly. Team will remain at base camp and regroup while assisting with ongoing efforts. Other groups have also been flown down and some awaiting heli flights with the hope that climbers and Sherpa from all teams are back in BC in the next few hours.

ALL SAFE IN BASE CAMP (April 27, 2015)

All climbers, guides, and Sherpa are now off the mountain and in Base Camp.

Today Fishtail Air, Manang Air, and Simrik Air are pulling together an incredible operation to heli everyone down from Camp I. If the weather holds as many as 160 climbers will be evacuated today. As we left Camp I, teams were arriving from Camp II after descending down the Western Cwm.

From SummitClimb website:

The season began in early April, after a snowy winter. The snow continued to fall lightly through the middle of April and the temperatures remained moderate. Our teams began arriving in Everest's Khumbu Valley at Lukla on 9 April. Our beautiful Himalayan trek arrived in basecamp on 17 April. While acclimatising at basecamp we completed a series of fun training walks, ice climbing practice sessions and forays into the mighty Khumbu icefall near basecamp. On 25 April, after lots of rest and training, we climbed the icefall during the wee hours up to camp 1 at 6100 metres / 20,000 feet.

Upon arriving in Camp 1, we had just retreated inside the tents when, at 12:00 noon, the glacier we were camped upon began violently rolling like the ocean and then a large avalanche let loose from the high above west ridge of Everest sweeping camp 1 with spindrfit snow and winds. We radioed down to basecamp to find out that a huge ice avalanche from Mount Lingtren had destroyed basecamp. Later we found out that that a 7.5 earthquake had hit the entire Nepal and Tibet region with as many as 10,000 people dead or missing. In basecamp around 20 people had been killed or were missing in the aftermath of the 1 minute earthquake. One of those tragically lost was Tom Taplin, a film maker who had chosen to not climb up to camp 1, but rather to stay in his tent in basecamp. He was later found hurled by high winds 300 metres / 500 feet from camp, near his broken camera. Our deepest condolences to the lost climber's family, friends, and colleagues. Needless to say, the route through the icefall was destroyed, with little possibility of safe descent and all 100 plus people in camp 1 were helicopter evacuated down to basecamp. Upon arrival we found basecamp to be leveled flat, a total loss.

All teams spent the next several days digging through the snow for their lost posessions, and then trekking and helicoptering back to Kathmandu.

From the Telegraph, Calcutta, India:

Guwahati, May 14: They were just 200 metres from where disaster struck - an avalanche that devoured 18 people at the Everest base camp.

The scars of April 25, when the tremors jolted Nepal, and still continue to do so, will be hard to erase, but the 11-member Assam Everest Expedition team has not lost hope of fulfilling a dream it has harboured for long-to climb the world's highest peak.

"It was around 11.45am when all of us could feel the glacier beneath our feet shaking violently. Thereafter, our worst fear became a reality as chunks of ice, stone and debris came crashing down at the base camp, demolishing everything in its way. We were training just 200 metres away," team leader Manash Barooah told reporters here today, recalling the "tryst with near death."

The team arrived in Guwahati yesterday after almost a month and a half since it left the city for Kathmandu en route to Base Camp.

The decision to cancel the expedition was taken on May 3, one that has left them shaken and distraught.

"The decision came after a meeting in the presence of several leaders of various expedition teams and Russell Brice, a representative of Himex, an organisation which guides mountaineering and trekking expeditions to Mt. Everest. Brice told us that he was running short of experienced Sherpas to open a route above Camp 2, as many of his Sherpas had left for home after the earthquake," Barooah said.

This large Everest/Lhotse expedition, with 36 climbing members and 30 climbing Sherpas, set up two camps above BC: C1 at the top of the Icefall and C2 in the Western Cwm, which were occupied by 10 Sherpas and 10 members. Bunce, Connally, Hess, Hota, Merle, Pastor, Penson, Polloczek, Pottinger and Williamson moved to C1 on 23 April and to C2 two days later, on the 25th.

Then at midday on the 25th a major earthquake struck the area. IMG's C2 was untouched, and its occupants stayed there for two nights; on the 27th they walked down to C1 and a helicopter picked them up and flew them to BC the same day.

Also on the 27th, the expedition's leadership decided to abandon the climb because of the risk of serious aftershocks. On the 29th, the expedition trekked to Lukla to wait for available flights to Kathmandu, where all of them had landed by 5 May.

ROUTEMEMO:
The Indian army mounted a large effort to scale Everest and Lhotse with 22 climbing members (16 Indian citizens and 6 Nepalese) from various army regiments, plus eight men from the Army Adventure Wing to assist in administration. Morever, two of the climbers, Ranveer Singh Jamwal and Hajari Lal, planned to summit Everest, descend to the South Col and summit Lhotse.

But despite the meticulous planning and climbers high hopes, it all came to naught when on 25 April a serious earthquake triggered fatal avalanching off of Pumori and Nuptse that devastated the Khumbu Valley. After-shocks continued for some time, and when this team left BC on 4 May, Pumori was still avalanching. A large part of Everest's base camp area was obliterated, but this team's camp was safe.

10 Sherpas had carried loads to C2 and marked out this team's camp area on 23 April. They were safely back in BC on the 25th. Jamwal, the leader, told his members on 27-28 April that he did not think it would be possible to do any more climbing this season, and when it was clear that no one would be refixing the route up to C2, he formally decided to abandon the climb on 4 May.

ROUTEMEMO:
Vajpai and the rest of the team were at ABC when the earthquake struck. After discussing the situation the following day, the team abandoned the expedition on 26 April.

The Seven Summit Treks members left by helicopter on 3 May.

Pavel Bem climbed together with Daniel de Gabai of Paulo Grobel's expedition. They started from C3. The weather was windy when they started, but improved during the course of 8th May. They stayed about 30 minutes on the summit and descended to C3, where Bem picked up his skis and skied down all the way to BC. He reached it at about 8 pm.

Paulo Grobel (on Chaar Baatsa Himal):

[After leaving Manaslu] we then trekked to Larkya Pass. Just to the north of the pass, southeast of Panbari, is a group of mountains including unclimbed Tsar Batsa Himal [Pawar Central] (6621m). Frank Bonhomme, Christian Hurgon, Dhane Magar, Deepen Bhote, Jangbu Sherpa, Rajan Lama and I headed up this peak from the pass with two tents and the minimum of equipment. Thanks to good acclimatization, weather, and snow conditions, we made rapid progress, and by midday had reached 5800m and a suitable campsite. The following morning, May 9, we left at 4 am, making quick progress up fine snow slopes until the sun softened the snow. The summit ridge had no nasty cornices, and we were on top at 11 am. Our ascent route, via a hanging glacier to the south, up onto the East Ridge, and so to the summit, was III/PD+.

The mountain was extremely dry and the weather was very hot this year. The way to BC required a lot of rope and was strenuous. As it was so warm, there was a lot of rockfall and it became apparent that the intended route (Ueli Steck in 2013) was impossible. When they reached their bivouac, they rested for a while and started to climb at 1 am. It was very warm and rocks were already falling in the middle of the night. When they reached a ledge, which was protected from the rockfall at 5800m, they stopped and spent the whole day there as it would have been too dangerous to go down in these conditions. They started their abseil at 5 pm, spent the night at bivouac 1 and descended to BC on 28 Sept.

Igonenc climbed to 5000m on the South Face to have a look, but he never intended to go to the summit.

Igonenc was the expedition leader for both the Annapurna I South Face and the Annapurna South East Ridge expeditions. He was listed only on the Annapurna I permit.

Kangshung, 6068m

We had hoped to attempt Chhuphu, an as far as we are aware unclimbed peak in the Thame valley. With issues in Kathmandu it became apparent that it would not be easy for us to gain a permit for this, so we sought an alternative objective. Richard at the Himalayan Database tipped us off about an "unclimbed" peak, Kangchung, located to the northwest of the Cho La Pass. The peak was opened for climbing by the Nepali Government in 2014, as part of the 104 new peaks released.

Richard let us know that two permits had been issued for the peak in the previous year, and through Iswari we contacted the agents who arranged the permits and discovered that neither team had been successful. After searching the Himalayan Index and the internet, and finding no records of attempts, we decided to attempt to make an ascent.

We initially recced that East Ridge of the mountain, which appeared to be fairly chossy, but worth an attempt. Unseasonal snowfall then made the slabby rock presented by this aspect of the mountain unattractive. We also looked at the North Face from the col on the Northeast Ridge, but this looked to consist of steep chossy slabs.


Will traversing across snow slopes after the ramp

Upon returning to the mountain after the storm we attempted the snowy West Ridge, but were turned around by sugary unconsolidated snow on steep ice/neve, giving dangerous conditions. The glacier that approaches the col to the west looked to be in tricky condition, so we avoided this by climbing a scree/snow ramp on the SW flank of the mountain. To access the col, traverse left at the top of the ramp.

We saw numerous signs of the passage of previous climbers on the mountain, including cairns and old water bottles. We have subsequently heard of three ascents by the West Ridge (all without permits), and one attempt on a route from the glacier to the north.

Late 80's - "Phil" soloed the West Ridge, finding easy snow slopes.

2006 - Local guide and lodge owner from Dragnag climbed the West Ridge, apparently finding it very straightforward.

2013- An acquaintance of Jon's summited from the west.

2014- A Czech team were rescued by helicopter whilst attempting to climb from the north side of the mountain.

A note to future potential ascentionists - the rock on this peak appears to be of poor quality.

Five members and four Sherpas left C3 for the summit at 4:45 am on 4 November, and got there at 9:00 am: the members descended to C1 between 5:00 and 7:00 pm in 40 km/hr wind which gave them clear but cold weather. Their Sherpas continued on down to BC the same day.

The successful members were Cherry, Dillier Degelo, Hill, Jarzebowski and Staples; the Sherpas were Ang Jangbu, Chewang Dorje, Da Nuru and Sange Dorje.

The three members who did not summit were Gillan, Heydon and Keaveney, who reached only as far C1. Heydon and Keaveney developed stomach trouble as they arrived at C1 on 1 November; they went back to BC the next day and stayed there until the climbing was over. Gillan got to C1 on 27 October, went back to BC the following day, received an e-mail message informing him that his father was dying and immediately left for home.

First the team went to acclimatize on Kyajo Ri SW Ridge normal route.

From Thami, they walked to Mende's monastery where they slept, then slept at 4550m BC. Then they slept at 5000m and at C2 at 5350m, both these days across a loose couloir. On summit day (6 Oct) they were faced with 150m rock grade 3, then a 500m snowy ridge (45/50 degrees steep maximum) to the top.

Hauer had to manage with all the rope fixing job. Sherpas being unable to help him, he had to fix alone, then down-climb to take back his ropes and so on. They should have been provided also with 700m of rope to equip the route safely, but they never had it.

All members with Da Tenji and Nima Tashi summited except Schrenk who was trekking around Mende's monastery.

Ama Dablam:

The entire team except Schrenk begun to climb the route on 12 Oct to ABC then C1 for deposits, but didn't sleep there. That day only Nima Tashi and Tashi Tshering pushed further to C2, then back to BC.

Sherpas decided they wouldn't fix the rope on the buttress between C2 and C3, arguing bad conditons. Rupert Hauer estimated (as for Kyajo Ri) that they did not have enough climbing skills and that their team hadn't been provided with enough rope to be fixed (1200m necessary, but were provided 800m only).

Due to the lack of personnel skills and also to unsecure appearing ice wall conditions above C3, Hauer decided to abandon the mountain on 13 Oct, went back up to C1 on 14 Oct to take back the equipment and would go next for Lobuche East instead.

Orywa, Huebschmann, Goebel and Schrenk left Ama Dablam back to Germany.

Lobuche East:

Sherpas claiming they had no insurances stopped at the wrong summit (according to Rupert Hauer, real summit is at 6119m whereas most teams stop at 6020m from which 2 French paragliders were seen taking off). All other members including leader summited on the real top that day.

ROUTE1: S Ridge up, SE Ridge down
SPONSOR: Ama Dablam South Ridge French Expedition
ROUTEMEMO:
BC  12/10  4600m
ABC 22/10  5600m
C1  23/10  6200m
C2  24/10  6600m
Smt 25/10  by all members.

Acclimatization on the SW Ridge normal route of Ama Dablam. Slept at ABC (5400m) on the 18 Oct, then the day after at C1 (5850m).

First alpine-style ascent of the South Ridge from 22 Oct to 25 Oct as follows:

22 Oct: group went to sleep afoot of the ridge at 5600m.

23 Oct on first day, the girls took the lead through some beautiful granite-like looking rock (found some crystals caves) pitches finding quite a few remnants old fixed ropes, this until Damien took the lead for the last 4 pitches. One notable pitch around 6100m in mushroom ice up to 4+/5 ice grade (some pitons for protection). Boys had some pictures from the previous 2005 Slovenian alpine-style attempt of the ridge showing a perfect ice cave around 6200m where they cold have slept all 4 together this time! But once arrived there the ice cave didn't exist anymore, so they had to push further up to 6300m to find a bivouac place to install their tents. Sebastien and Fleur slept seated, Damien and Fanny above them hardly laying down (Fanny fell during the night, was unfortunately hooked to the belay). Little snow fall during that evening.

24 Oct on second day, the boys with their heavy bags alternately took the lead. First pitch through an exposed straight-side traverse, then pitches through a 250m high 45-degree steep couloir (slightly steeper at the end). Then Sebastien led first through the exit ice ramp with a first pitch at 70/75 degrees, then Damien in a 60-degree bad ice one. During that section, they could again find some other remnants of old fixed ropes. Eventually Damien led through a 3 ice flutes precarious section (however with good ice for protection in the holes between it) before the proper exit presenting itself as an ice fall with difficulties up to 90 degrees on a very few meters (remnants of old fixed rope on the right side of it fixed to a rock pinnacle), both these sections representing an overall 80m pitch. On top of it at 6600m had a cold windy (SW direction) bivouac on the SW ridge.

25 Oct: start at 7:30 am from C2, summit at 10:30 am in perfect conditions (no wind, clear sky). Here they met with an Austrian guide. Then they went down the SW Ridge back to base camp at 6 pm.

On way down, were shocked to see bunch of remnant fixed ropes and lack of security of new one fixed anarchically under ultimate responsibility of foreign agencies. Equipped themselves their own belay of abseils.

French grade for the route is TD+ with quite continuous difficulties up to IV rock and ice 5, with some delicate ice flute mushroom section. Overall impression: "little harder than the Brown Patey route of Aiguille sans nom in Massif du Mont Blanc which is a gentle TD+".

Possible variant to the final ramp by an upper one may be taken by Jeff Lowe during his solo ascent? Exact very final part of the South Ridge unclimbed and seeming extremely difficult.
ACCIDENTS: Slight frostbite of right-hand index finger of Damien Tomasi
ACHIEVMENT: 1st alpine style ascent of 'Lagunak' South Ridge of Ama Dablam

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6810m due to high winds
ROUTEMEMO:
Approach: Lukla, Hinku Valley, Sutsaki La, Panggom, Cholem Kharka, Khote, Khare, Mera Peaks, Kongmedigma, Baruntse BC

BC  12/10  5450m
C1  20/10  6150m (just above W Col)
C2  25/10  6400m (just at small plateau on SE ridge)
HPt 27/10  6810m(GPS) Robert, Zac, Keith.

Climb abandoned on 28 Oct due to high wind (risk of frostbite)
Fixed rope: 270m until C1

Summited wrong and main peaks of Mera on 10 Oct. 3 peaks there most people go the central one, whereas they went on this one plus the real northern one, lying 600m further and 30m higher (2 hours to go and back from wrong top). Little bit exposed with little slab avalanching risk. Real summit for Zac, Bob Killip, Keith Scott. 4 others stopped at the wrong summit including Alan Coldwell (not on Baruntse permit).

Then they arrived at the empty Baruntse BC (other teams would arrive later). So did all the rope fixing work up to the West Col C1 on their own.

First tried to find a straighter route to the West Col broad face by a couloir. After 2 days fixing it, bad surprise came as it was impossible from its top to walk further right onto the snow, that one forming like a huge scoop.

Went back up to remove these ropes on 17 Oct. On that day on descent, Zac was almost killed by a big rock fall, lukily only cutting his head (bleeding). So instead they continued by the standard route, equipping it while descending.

Next day on 18 Oct, Zac flew to Lukla for some medical check, then flew back on the same day to the BC.

26 Oct Bob and Zac put some fixed rope up to 6600m.

27 Oct Bob, Zac, Keith tried for the summit. Facing strong wind (50 kh/hr during last 3 days) and knowing there was much distance remaining to the top, knew they could not make it without severe frostbite risk. Stopped at 6810m (GPS reading), then went back to C1.

28 Oct back to BC.

30 Oct left BC by Amphu Laptsa.

03 Nov back to Lukla.

SPONSOR: Enam Chhopa Bamare French Expedition Autumn 2015
TERMREASON: Bad conditions (deep snow, avalanching, falling ice, or rock)
TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 5200m due dry conditions and unstable weather conditions
ROUTEMEMO:
BC  13/10    4100m
C1  20-22/10 4900m
HPt 21/10    5200m     
	 
Climb abandoned on 23 Oct 10 due tomountain dry conditions and unstable weather conditions.

Youngsters team of French Mountaineering Federation (FFME) coached by Antoine Pecher (FFME) and Dimitry Munoz (French military group, GMHM). 

First expedition to explore Chhopa Bamare southeastern valley mountaineering possibilities. Had tried to plan at best the expedition, but few pictures of the mountain, used Googl Earth to estimate their BC location plus some pictures of the Chinese side of the mountain. Knew they would have to define once in situ their climbing objectives.

Approach:

The expedition first met during approach march with post-earthquake effects (epicenter was close from the area) having to trek on the almost completely destroyed road usually linking Gongar to Lamabagar, where they stayed 3 nights. They found also remnants of Chinese workers campsites who went there for the construction of a future dam (800 inhabitants including dam workers before the earthquake, only 70 after).

Team put 2 camps in the jungle to reach its base camp at the footstep of the upper valley summits. Also met with village people there and some military guards, the first with whom they had a Buddhist ceremony. Locals had seen so far only a Japanese party trekking the main valley and no westerners at all since the earthquake.

Expedition time:

Weather was like a clear sky every day then clouds/fog coming from 10 am. Found mountain not in good condition, with a lots of rock falls every day. Would have needed more snow and permafrost to keep ground more secure to progress. Chose instead to try solutions on the borders of the valley.

On 19 Oct the team had tried from BC to access an eastern 5700m attractive subpeak. First following some scarce trails, the group then used a thalweg, seeking for some solutions to access another valley giving access to Chhopa Bamare itself. Despite GE and maps preliminary research allowing some hope, reality on the ground showed only proper rock cliffs not allowing to go further. So they stopped around 4300m, just nearby above base camp. Other tries around in the previous days reaching some hills point kairned by yak herders or locals.

On 20 Oct the entire group went to C1. From there in the days coming, different parties tried different solutions west side of the whole valley without success.

High point on 21 Oct. That day 2 teams attempted respectively 1 secondary summit ridge (Antoine Pecher and Antoine Lang). 1 snowy ramp giving access to the same ridge (Dimitry, Yohan, Kevin, Renaud, third team seeking for a col on the border with China (Mael, Baguet, Eric). First team abandoned facing dangerous rotten rock section at about 5200m. That day Yohan and Kevin went back to BC.

On 22 Oct Eric and Dimitry also went to BC. Others reached a lake close to the border. On Friday 23 Oct remnant 4 down to BC. That day they went up a 4900m hill above C1, the only so far without any kairns on it.

Globally consider the area interesting for mountaineering, despite quickly facing with committed ground, with long ridges in unstable rock (loose granite). Best conditions for attempts would be snowy one to give cohesion to the rocks. Best solution probably on the right side of the face, trying to link some rockfall exposed gullies in the lowest part to north Eastern ridge in the upper one.

Sherpas porter team arrived on 25 Oct, all team left BC on 26 Oct. On return on 27 Oct at Lamabagar, could watch a massive rock fall showing ground still not stable. Then at Gongar, all group felt a notable earthquake effect. Team put 100m of fixed rope during return march between Lamabagar and Gongar.

Eric Jamet going to guide on Ama Dablam.

Renaud and Antoine to help for reconstruction.

All other members going back to France.
ACHIEVMENT: 1st expedition in the SE valley of Chhopa Bamare

ROUTEMEMO:
BC  28/09  5000m
C1  17/10  6100m
C2  18/10  6600m
Smt 19/10  by all 3 members.

Team acclimatized by summiting Hongku peak via the SW Ridge on 9 Oct from a previous bivouac at 6200m.

Checked during 2 weeks conditions on the North Face. Had a try on the east side of the face, after spending a night at 5500m on the 4 Oct the day after reached 6000m at an hanging glacier where they were stopped by bad weather and snow conditions.

After that just watched the western part of the face, but considering the apparent continous long difficulties of the route with no proper places for tents, didn't try anything. This despite also considering the route being not too much exposed to potential objective dangers (no hanging seracs there).

After 6 days of bad weather, the trio started to climb on the SW Ridge.

17 Oct: from base camp to C1 at 6100m. No difficulties.

18 Oct: roped all day to go on the sharp snowy ridge up to a second biv at 6600m.

19 Oct: after a night in not so good weather conditions, eventually decided to start at 8 am. Meeting some steep pitches, reached the top at 12:30 pm, then were back to sleep at their last camp at 2:30 pm.

2O Oct: all members went back to base camp, reaching it at 4 pm.
ACHIEVMENT: 2nd alpine-style ascent of the W Ridge (last one in 1992)

ROUTEMEMO:
BC  27/10  5300m
Bv1 28/10  6500m
Bv2 29/10  6400m
Smt 28/10  by Mingma Gyalje Sherpa at 5:15 pm.

Took 2 60m ropes, 2 Cobra ice axes and no bivouac gear.

On 28 Oct, started 4 am, climbed up a 200m long rocky ridge, then down-climbed 60m to reach the snow. Proper beginning of the face at 5:30 am, with first 100m little step (45 degrees), then straight up (continous 75 to 80 degrees all the way up). At 2 places created some small platforms to stay and relax, otherwise no place to put a tent. Ice covered by snow two layers, so had to climb "on a lateral way", never straight to avoid to destablilize this particular snow layers.

Reached summit of the West Face at 5 pm, then main summit at 5:15 pm through a flat ridge (not corniced). First tried to descend by the South Ridge, but after a while trapped in complete white-out. So waited a little, but nothing happened. Tried to move down on the SE side, then tried shelter under a little ice serac at around 6500m reached around 6:30 pm. Spent one night there with no tent, food, gas, water nothing! Cold night waiting for the day.

On 29 Oct weather still cloudy! Around 11:30 am, with little visibility could see 2 small mountains in front of him, but couldn't recognize it despite being his homeland. So at some ice serac just followed an azimuth and thus went 100m down. At the end of it, didn't see he was reaching soem snow ice overhanging border: his legs breaking it, he fell down some 10 feet. Quite shocked after that, he just traversed from there some 10 horizontal meters to avoid the overhang above him.  2nd night without equipment curled up to economize his energy, that night becoming windy compared with the previous one. During the night, in newly clear sky conditions could see everything (was looking his watch all night long), but about 4 am weather went covered again! Around 6 am, went a little visible, made phone calls to his friends for helicopter rescue, but no hope helicopter could pick him up in these cloudy conditions. Stayed there anyway feeling like "If I go down I just have to die." Luckily helicopter came around 7:30 am, [and took him] back to Na 5 minutes later. The day after on 31 Oct he flew back to Kathmandu by helicopter for some minor cares in CIWEC hospital (one blister on a finger foot)

First ever technical solo ascent by a Nepali Sherpa. In his website, Mingma considers it however as his most dangerous climb and also his biggest mistake! Thought he could climb the face up and down within the same day.

This project was otherwise part of a wider project built with 3 other Sherpas friends of Rolwaling, aiming to climb 3 mountains in 3 days (actually done: Langdak, Raungsiyar and Thakargo East), plus this solo ascent of Chobutse. Goal was to make hobby more popular amongst younger Nepalis and also make Rolwaling better known. Mingma thinks mountaineering so far was just being a professional activity for older Nepalis. Youngsters now begin to enjoy it more as a hobby, doing ice and rock climbing by themselves.

Next: Nanga Parbat in summer 2016 for Mingma, as his mother forbids him to try it in winter.
ACHIEVMENT: 1st ascent of West Face of Chobutse; 1st solo ascent by a Sherpa of a technical route
