ROUTEMEMO:
We placed the base camp on the south side near the two lake's. The altitude was 5300m. We reached BC on 26 April 2017. Before starting, we studied carefully the route that the Croats made in the 80's. They chose a variant on the left side and went out into the south-western ridge.

We decided to try a direct route on the central part of the wall and make the exit to the central ramp.

We had the same start as the Croatians, but we lost a day at the entrance on the face. The glacier is fractured by a giant crevase that we have hardly managed to cross it.

After entering the common chimney, we took it on a direct couloir, avoiding the left side of the wall. We didn't find any equipment or materials in the wall. We climb for 4 days in very difficult conditions and we placed 3 bivy's. The route is mainly composed by rock and ice at 70-90 degrees. We reached 6580m on the last bivy, and we were close to the exit in the summit ridge, but around 5 am, black clouds swallowed the face, soon it start snowing heavily, we treat climbing and we reached the highest point on 6800m where an avalanche took me down and I stopped by one Friend. I was very lucky.

We reached 6800m, and after the avalanche fell on the ramp, we realised we are like sitting ducks in front of fire, so considering the weather, we decided that the only chance we have, is to belay down the wall. Although I did not hit in fall I started coughing hard enough (our bivy platform at 6580m was gone and the wall has transformed into a huge avalanche funnel).

We started belying in regroups made from a single piton (we had 13 CT pitons with us, we used 11 and we also left some slings and friends in some regroups). I don't really know exactly what hour we reached the base of the wall, but I know it was already night. We reached BC in 10 May around 2-3 am. We were completely finished.
ACCIDENTS: Fall without incidence (just some snow particules in the lungs)

Note from Paulo Grobel - 6 August 2017

After many expeditions to Himlung Himal by various itineraries, I felt confident to try a big journey at altitude, traversing this accessible 7000er via a previously untrodden ridge, carrying more than four days of food. Our plan was to climb the normal route up the NW Ridge of Himlung Himal (7126m), without fixed ropes of course, put a high camp just below the summit, then climb over the top with food and all our gear and continue to the unclimbed Himlung East (6932m). The latter is a recently opened (2014) peak on the frontier with Tibet, on the ridge that leads north toward Ratna Chuli. We would then continue down this north ridge, over several tops including Phu Kang (6694m; opened in 2002, this peak is still unclimbed, though the goal of a 2008 Swiss expedition that found no accessible route to the summit), and onward to the vicinity of Peak 6566m, where we would descend the ridge west, and eventually reach the valley north of Himlung Himal, so completing a "horseshoe."

It was a strange climb. We established five camps on the NW Ridge of Himlung Himal, the last on a flat shoulder at 7050m, northwest of and not far from the main top. It was higher than our eventual goal, Himlung East. We arrived at this camp at 11 am on May 7: the previous day two members of our group, Jean Francois Males and Karma Sherpa, had reached the main summit. However, the weather on the 7th was not good. Bertrand Vayssettes and I climbed to the main summit to scope the way ahead. We had spent a lot of time acclimatizing so we could live safely at 7000m and our schedule was now tight. More importantly, the weather felt too unstable to commit to the full traverse. Reluctantly, we took the decision to make Himlung East our goal.

On the 8th Isabelle Guillaume with Jangbu Sherpa, and Rajan Bhote and I left camp and skirted the summit by the northern slopes to reach the east ridge, which we descended, narrow at first, to a col and then ascended the west ridge of Himlung East to its summit. On the return we climbed up the east ridge of Himlung Himal and over the summit, so making the first ascent of this ridge. That same day five other members of the group reached the top of Himlung Himal. By the time we had returned to camp the weather was poor again, but fortunately next morning the cloud level rose and we were able to make our descent. Traversing a summit is such a great game, this ridge is fantastic, and the continuation north is still there to be done.

Team 1 started for the summit from C4 at 11 pm on 27 May. They stayed on the top for 40 minutes. Weather was very good on that day, clear, bright and sunny on the summit. When they started there were strong winds, but later on winds subsided. They were the last team on Everest, making use of the very last weather window. Team got back to C4 around noon and to C2 after 1 hour rest. They reached C2 at 5:30 pm. Next day to BC (29 May).

Team 2 on summit push left C4 at 11:30 pm on 26 May. It was cloudy, windy, visibility was very poor. Wind turned to a storm on the summit (40km/h). Team stayed on the top for at least 30 minutes. They got back to C4 at 12:17 pm. After 1 hour rest, team continued descent to C2, which they reached at 7:30 pm. Next day to BC (28 May).

Monti Rajkhowa from Team 1 was not able to make a summit attempt, she had a bad cough and sore throat and abandoned climb at C2. She got as high as C4 on first summit attempt, but had to go down again because of bad weather. 

Surendra Tomar from Team 2 abandoned climb at C2 because of abdominal pain. He reached C3 on the first rotation. Tomar was evacuated from C2 to BC by helicopter on 25 May.

The expedition collected 500 kg of garbage from South Col, bringing it down to C2. Helicopter took the garbage from C2 to BC. 1000 kg of garbage was collected at BC.

On Lhotse summit push Skatov left C4 at 8 am. It was warm, good weather. Skatov's Sherpa Lhakpa Dendi turned around after 50m from C4, Skatov continued alone. At noon he had a problem, his regulator did not work properly and he continued to the summit with a very low oxygen rate. He felt pain in his back, maybe caused by low oxygen. He stayed on the top for 30 minutes, weather and view were perfect. 

He descended to C4 reaching it at 8:15 pm. He reached the South Col with Lhakpa Dendi Sherpa on the same day at 11:15 pm. But on the South Col the wind started to increase and they returned to C4 on Lhotse. At midnight they arrived at the camp. On 18 May they descended to C2, staying there for 3 nights. 

On 21 May Skatov and Lhakpa Dendi went from C2 to C3. A new Sherpa (Pasang) arrived to support them. On 22 May they ascended to C4 (South Col). At 7:30 pm Skatov and Pasang started their summit push. Lhakpa Dendi followed them a little bit later. Pasang and Skatov reached the summit of Everest at 5 am (23 May). They stayed on the top for 35 minutes. It was very cold and windy. Lhakpa reached the summit 10 minutes after Skatov and Pasang. Skatov descended alone as he was faster than Pasang and Lhakpa Dendi and didn't want to wait for them. Lhakpa Dendi slept at C4, Skatov went down to C2, reaching the camp at 6 pm. On 24 May Skatov reached BC and left 25 May be helicopter.

Skatov is vegan.

Lhotse summit team left C2 at 8 am as the rope-fixing Sherpas were in front of them. They caught up with the rope-fixing Sherpas at about 8200m. The weather was calm and warm, so waiting for the Sherpas was not too bad. There were 17 people on the route (including rope-fixing Sherpas).

On 20 May the Everest summit team went back to C3 and 21 May to C4. On 21 May they left C4 at 8 pm and after they had climbed for about 4 hours, they heard on the radio that a Pakistani (Bhatti) and his Sherpa were in trouble and had called for help. They discussed it and cancelled their summit push for Temba to help rescue. Sefland went back to C4. 

On 22 May Temba and a group of other Sherpas came back to C4. A doctor of Madison Mountaineering injected Dex into him and even though she said that he was about 5% alive, he was back talking again after 3 hours. As Temba was sort of organizing this rescue, he said to Sefland that he would no longer go to the summit with her, but organise another Sherpa.

On 22 May Sefland and Pasang (he was on the Everest rope-fixing team on 15 May) left C4 at 9:30 pm. It was very windy, Sefland thought it would have been too windy, but Madison Mountaineering had a big team going up there, so she trusted that it would be ok. 

They spent about 20 minutes on the summit and then descended to C4. When she got to the South Summit Sefland noticed that she had developed slight HAPE, so she was very aware of this. When she arrived there at about 4 pm, she rested a bit, got an injection of dexamethasone and then took nifidepine in pills. They arrived at C2 at 10:30 pm. On 24 May she descended to BC.

Bhatti and Sange left C4 at 7 pm on 20 May. At the Balcony they replaced their old oxygen with fresh oxygen bottles. They felt good at that point. Suddenly the weather deteriorated, a lot of wind, freezing their oxygen masks and goggles. Sange wanted to turn around, but Bhatti insisted to continue to the summit. They summited on 21 May in the morning. They spent 5 minutes on the summit, Sange felt dizzy and was not able to see clearly. They ran out of oxygen and Bhatti was descending very slowly. It got dark and they rested after a long descent near the Balcony. Sange tried to talk to Bhatti, but he didn't answer and seemed to be unconscious. They had no food or water. Sange fell asleep as well, he woke up again by the noise of ascending climbers. His hands were completely frozen, he couldn't move his body. 

Seven Summit Treks organized a rescue team of six Sherpas led by Temba Bhote from C3. Ang Tsering Sherpa and Nima Gyalzen (Sherpa Khangri) were the first to reach Bhatti and Sange in the morning on 22 May. They moved them down to C4. Bhatti and Sange were near death. They were stuck at C4 for a while because of bad weather. Next day on 23 May they moved Bhatti with great difficulty from C4 to C3. On 24 May Bhatti was brought from C3 to BC by helicopter. And Sange was left at C3 for one night on his own, according to Nima Gyalzen.

Mohammed was acclimatizing very slowly. He only made it to C3. When he heard that his partner Bhatti was in trouble, he decided to stay in C2 to coordinate the rescue and to not ascend any further. According to Mohammed, Sange was young and inexperienced: he didn't bring enough oxygen.

Bhatti is in hospital in Kathmandu undergoing treatment.

  Taken and used by Bhatti and Sange from C3 to summit (they ran out of oxygen
  during the descent between the summit and the Balcony.

On the descent Nima Gyalzen, Ang Chhiring Lama, Jangbu Sherpa and Mingma Chhiri Sherpa at about 8700m they found Bhatti, the Pakistani, and Sange Sherpa at about 3 am. Bhatti was asleep and Dawa Sange Sherpa 95% dead. He had thrown off his gloves and oxygen and was hanging on the rope. Nima Gyalzen removed his oxygen and his spare mask and gave it to Bhatti and Dawa Sange Sherpa. 

Nima Gyalzen put both sick people on the rope and the four Sherpas dragged them down to C4, where they arrived at 3 pm. There the doctor of IMG gave Bhatti and Sange Sherpa dexamethasone. 

On 23 May Seven Summit Sherpas brought them to C3, from where Bhatti was long-lined to BC and then to Kathmandu and Dawa Sange Sherpa was left at C3 on his own for the night. On 24 May he was brought down to C2 and heli-vaced to Kathmandu.

ROUTEMEMO:
Nirmal Purja summited Everest twice and Lhotse once, before coming to Makalu.

On 30 May in the early morning, Purja took a helicopter back to Lukla from Namche, picked up four Sherpas and flew to Makalu BC, where they arrived at 7:41 am.

ABC 30/05  5700m (7:41 am)
C1  30/05  6650m (5:20 pm, spent the night there)
C2  31/05  7450m (5:30 pm, spent a few hours there)
Smt 01/06  by Purja, Walung Dorje, Mingma Dorje and Jangbu at 9:20 am. 

They left ABC at 10:15 am to C2, arrived at 5:20 pm. It took them a relatively long time because they had to break trail in knee-deep snow. They used the existing fixed ropes on the headwall below C2 and up to the Makalu La. They carried 300m of rope, ice screws and snowbars and tents. 

Left C2 at 9 am. They were not using oxygen up to about 7400m, but Purja decided to put everyone on oxygen from there as they were going to slowly. They reached C3 at 5:30 pm. They pitched their tent. The weather was horrendous. Very windy again. 

Purja, Mingma Dorje, Jangbu and Walung Dorje left C3 at 10:30 pm. They went up via C4, but lost the route for a bit and did not use the lower traverse to the French couloir. They traversed across higher up. At 7:26 am they reached the couloir. The rope in the French Couloir was still usable and was not as buried as on the rest of the route.

They spent about 15 minutes on the summit and descended to C3, where they arrived at 1:30 pm. Rested for a few hours and descended to ABC, where they arrived at 5:30 pm.

On 2 June they waited for a helicopter, but no heli due to bad weather. On 3 June they left ABC by foot at 3 pm and after having walked the whole night, they arrived at Num at 9 am the following day. From Num to Tumlingtar by jeep on 4 June and back to Kathmandu on 4 June.

Oxygen: taken and used from 7400m to summit to C2 by all.

Summit 20 May: Salte, Anthony Sanchez, Kjoelvik, Yearwood and Borang arrived at C4 on 17 May. The weather was not good (windy and snowing) and they stayed at the South Col until 19 May. They left C4 at 7:30 pm on 19 May. Summiters stayed for about 20 minutes on the summit and descended to C4, where they arrived at 2 pm.

Sanchez turned around at the Hillary Step he was too tired. 

Yearwood reached the Balcony at about 5:30 am. His Sherpa Sange told him to go down as he was so slow, but Yearwood insisted on continuing to the South Summit (8700m), where they arrived at about 3 pm. Yearwood was very confused and finally agreed to go down. He started to descend, but Sange ended up dragging him down for about 2 hours. They reached the Balcony at 7:30 pm. The weather was bad, Yearwood showed severe signs of HACE. He threw his gear around, rolled in the snow, took off his oxygen mask. He died at midnight. Sange stayed with him until he died and descended to C4, where he arrived at 1:30 am on 21 May.

On 21 May and 24 May the Sherpas went back up to the Balcony to recover Yearwood's body; however, both times the weather did not allow for a recovery.

Mito Borang reached the Balcony (8400m), but the weather was too bad for him and his Sherpa told him to turn back as he was too slow. He decided to turn back to C4 and abandon the climb.

Kjoelvik reached C4 on 17 May. He was very sick up there and descended to C2 on 19 May.

On 21 May summit team and Hanna Bruss, Sylwia Bajek, Damian Bourke, Krzysztof Sabisz, Szczepan Brzeski left C4 at 7:30 pm. Bruss reached the Balcony, but she was very slow and very tired and descended to C4 with Lhakpa Gelu [from British Gurka team]. Bajek and Brzeski reached the Hillary Step and decided to go down as their oxygen was running low. It was 11 am. They had insisted on climbing on a high flow rate, even though they were told that they would not reach the summit with such a high flow rate (by Pema Renzi). They descended to C4 and stayed the night. They had enough oxygen to sleep on it at a flow rate of 1/2 litre.

Damian Bourke got hit by a rock or an oxygen bottle on his way to the summit. He was in the rock field below the Balcony (8300m). He got knocked over and descended to C4, where he stayed the night and on 22 May, he descended to C2 from where he was helicoptered back to Kathmandu.

Sabisz reached the South Summit. He was very slow and sick. His Sherpa told him to come down. He descended to C4 with slight HACE, spent the night at C4, on 22 May to C2 and on 23 May to BC.

Summit team descended to C4, where they spent the night.

Kress reached C3 on 29 April; he left the expedition when he heard about Ueli Steck's death.

Mori reached BC but was so dehydrated, the doctor at the HRA clinic gave her two litres of saline at BC. She left the expedition on 20 April. 

Rigney reached C2 and left expedition due to his bad cough.

Mazur stayed at BC as he never intended to climb.

When Niebuhr reached the Balcony he got a pain in his left shoulder, which is probably due to the fact that he can only use one arm. The other one has been paralyzed since he was 8 years old (it happened after a brain surgery). He was thinking of whether to continue or not, but then he saw a dead body on the Balcony and was convinced that he should go down. He thought that he could make it to the top, but probably not back down again. They descended to C4, slept for a few hours and then continued to C2, where they arrived at about noon. Hudson was on his way to C4, when at about 7700m he felt absolutely exhausted. He had no power left. He spoke to his Sherpa, Abirail Rai, who told him that it was still be four hours to get to the summit. Hudson was feeling so tired and so slow that he decided to turn back to C3, where they arrived at 6 pm. They stayed there for the night. On 21 May, he descended to C2 the following day and developed a strange cough, coughing up bits of gunk. On 22 May, he went to BC. Hpt 20/05  reached by Birn near South Summit 6 am; by Pariz at Balcony at 5 am;
           by Braillard at 8500m.

On their summit push, Birn and Pariz left C4 at 9 pm on 19 May. Braillard left around midnight, because he was tired when he arrived at C4 earlier that day and needed some more rest before summit push. 

They were promised by their agency new oxygen bottles at the Balcony, but when Pariz and Birn reached the Balcony, there was no oxygen. Birn and Pariz realized that they would not have enough oxygen to summit and descend safely and decided to turn around. Birn turned around near South Summit, Pariz at the Balcony. 

They had problems with their Sherpas as well: communication was bad, the climbers felt cheated by their Sherpas and agency. Birn got back to C4 at 8:30 am, Pariz at 6:30 am. Birn's Sherpa had left him alone earlier and was waiting at C4 when he arrived. Birn had to descend alone and he was in bad condition, suffering from frostbite at extremities and having problems with his vision as he was only able to see on one eye.

Conditions were excellent, a perfect summit day, the team had felt in great mental and physical condition. Had they had the oxygen they were promised by their agency, Pariz and Birn are convinced they would have reached the summit. Birn and Pariz met at C4 and descended to C3, reaching it at 3 pm. Next day, they were rescued by helicopter from C2.

ROUTEMEMO:
Ueli Steck died 30 April on Nuptse from falling during an acclimatization climb.

From Ueli Steck family media release:

On April 29, 2017, Ueli Steck ascended from the Everest base camp to camp 2 on about 6400 meters above sea level. His original plan was to climb the next day for further acclimatization on the Everest traditional route to the almost 8000 meters high south col, in order to return to camp 2 on the same day.

From camp 2, Ueli noted that the conditions in the Nuptse wall were ideal, which is why he decided in the evening to change his plan and to climb up to Nuptse the following day.

On April 30, 2017, Ueli started at 4:30 am. Together with the Frenchman Yannick Graziani, he crossed the gaping glacier. Afterwards, Graziani continued on the Everest normal route towards Camp 3, while Ueli entered into the Lhotse flank. Ueli's accident occurred on around 7600 meters at about 09.00 (local time). His body was finally recovered by the Italian helicopter pilot Maurizio Folini at a height of about 6600 meters and transferred to the hospital of Katmandu.

The cause of the accident is still unknown.

In the following article, journalist and climber, Billi Bierling, pays tribute to her friend Ueli Steck, the Swiss elite climber who died on Sunday on an Everest expedition. 

Ueli Steck: Climbing in the here and now

Since I first met Ueli in 2007 I got to know him rather well - partially because of the Himalayan Database (former journalist Elizabeth Hawley's authoritative record of mountaineering expeditions since the early 20th century to more than 300 significant Nepalese peaks) and the work I do for ‘Miss Hawley’ and partially on a private basis.

What I liked about Ueli was his modesty and the fact that despite his amazing achievements he was down to earth - never arrogant. When you look at the CV on his website, it says: Profession: “trained carpenter” even before he lists all his incredible mountaineering achievements.

It was always a pleasure to see him in Kathmandu and talk to him about his climbs for the records of Miss Hawley's Himalayan Database. Every once in a while, I also interviewed him as a journalist, and in particular for swissinfo.ch. He always took the time to talk to me, even though he hardly ever stayed in Kathmandu for longer than 24 hours.

I remember the incident with the Sherpas in 2013. At the time, I was at Everest Base Camp and of course, I received a phone call that I wasn't necessarily looking forward to from Dale Bechtel, editor of the English service of swissinfo.ch, asking me whether I could interview Ueli. I did not want to bother Ueli with this as he was more to me than just a ‘subject’ for a good story - he was a friend. But still - I am a journalist at heart and so I went to see him. At first he said that he would not talk to the media but in the evening he came over to our camp and told me that he trusted me and that he would talk to me. I really appreciated it and the conversation revealed how shaken he had been by the whole event.

‘Swiss Machine’ label

One thing Ueli did not particularly like was the term ‘Swiss Machine.’ He was branded with it, but he never warmed to this description of his abilities; so reading it all over the media now makes me almost sad. He was much more than a machine – he was a kind, down-to-earth and rather introverted person.

We all know that he was extraordinary - I don't need to go into this. But I feel fortunate that I got to know Ueli personally and was lucky enough to enjoy his trust. I remember after he had got back from the South Face of Annapurna I, it did not take very long for the first doubters to emerge. I was trekking in the Khumbu when I received an email from a journalist trying to dig up some dirt about the ascent.

It really shocked me that so shortly after this impressive climb, people were already trying to break him. I sent him a quick text to warn him and he just replied: “Thanks - it's great to have friends like you.” It makes me very sad that to this very day, people doubt his ascent of Annapurna I and of course, only Ueli knows whether he reached the top or not. But both Miss Hawley and I have always believed him as we are convinced that he was absolutely capable of doing this. I think a lot of the doubts came from the fact that people did not think it was feasible to do - but with Ueli it was. He made the impossible possible.

Annapurna chapter

When I heard about this tragic accident on Sunday morning, I was in the midst of translating his latest book, which I have been working on for the past two months. Ueli and I were very excited that one of his books was finally going to be published in the English-speaking world as he had always wanted to have an English version of one of his works. So I have lived and breathed Ueli for the past two months. I would like to share an excerpt from the Annapurna South Face chapter, which I re-read several times after I had heard about his accident. Maybe this section can help us better understand, where Ueli was coming from.

Book excerpt

“I was completely detached from the other world. There was nothing else but climbing. No goal, no future, no past. I was climbing in the here and now. One swing of the ice axe after the other; one step after the other. I only saw my ice axes and how they penetrated the snow and ice. My view narrowed and I had adopted some sort of tunnel vision. And here I was; in the middle of this gigantic face with very limited equipment. I felt light, but also extremely exposed. I knew that the tiniest mistake would mean certain death. However, I was not scared of making a mistake. I was still giving orders and controlling the person climbing the south face of Annapurna. It did not feel like me. If this person fell, it would not really concern me.”

I remember when I once asked him how he could possibly not be scared when scaling such gigantic steep faces without a rope, he responded: “Climbing is like walking the stairs. I never expect to slip and fall. Do you?” And this is exactly how he viewed climbing - like walking the stairs. But unfortunately, on Sunday these stairs became fatal for him. It is a great loss for the climbing community; a great loss for his friends and family and of course a huge loss for his wife Nicole. My thoughts are with all of them and I will certainly remember Ueli as an amazing climber, a good friend and an inspiration for a lot of people. Despite his fervor for speedy ascents, he never lost his love and passion for the mountains.

Davy arrived in Kathmandu on 17 March. Spent two days in Kathmandu to buy some equipment. Bussed to Jiri. Trekked from Jiri to Namche. He carried his own rucksack, which weighed 42kg all the way to Surke. The bag broke and then he realized that he needed a porter. They trekked to Lobuche BC together. On 29 March, they arrived there and he climbed to C1 30 March in the morning. Wanted to do it in the dark not to be seen. He summited Lobuche on 2 Aril.

After Lobuche, he spent a few weeks walking up and down Kalapattar and Pumori C1 to acclimatize. Every seven days, he would run back to Namche to get some provisions. It took him a day from Pumori BC to Namche, spend a night in Namche and then go back. He did this about 4 times. 

On 2 May, he left Pumori BC at about 7 pm, to Gorak Shep and Everest BC at about 8:30 pm and returned to Pumori BC. It was a very dark night. The reason he did that was to break trail as it had been snowing. On 3 May, he left his tent at Pumori BC at 3 am and reached EBC at 4:14 am. There were about 3 or 4 flashlights in the Khumbu Icefall. As he was climbing illegally, he waited for the next person to enter the Icefall to follow as he wanted to appear as part of a team. It was about 4:30 am. When he reached C1 at 10:30 am he realized that all the tents had expedition names on them and that his tent would stick out. He decided to descend and come up with a good idea of how to get a name on his tent. He left C1 at 1:30 pm. It took him 3 hours to descend. The weather turned bad and it started graupeling (snowing with soft pellets). 

As he saw the SPPC tent in the Icefall he stayed in the Icefall a bit to make sure that he was not discovered. At about 5:30 pm he walked through BC and he sensed that there was going to be an issue as he was wearing his 8000m-boots and he knew that it would be obvious if he walked out of base camp wearing these boots. He got out of BC and four men whistled to attract his attention. He waved and as soon as he was around a rock he made for a little cave. He sat in the cave for about 10 minutes and as he peered around the corner to check out where they were, they came around the corner and discovered him. One of them was Phurba Tashi and another Gyanendra Shresthat (from the Ministry of Tourism). 

They told him to get out of the cave and show them his passport, which he miraculously had with him. They asked him whether he had a permit and he admitted he did not. They went to the Satori Camp and they gave him tea and biscuits and discussed the situation. Davy admitted everything, including Lobuche and Pumori. They told him to leave BC immediately and kept his passport. He went back to his tent and he was very down as he had the feeling that he had failed his mission.
During the night, he realized that he did not have any details of the people who took his passport so he returned to BC. He met Russell Brice, who told him to look for the liaison officer. Davy found Gyanendra, who had arrested him. There were two Sherpas in the tent and one Sherpa was very aggressive toward him and was physical abusive. He slapped him in the face and took pictures of him. The Sherpa then threw him toward Gyanendra. The Sherpa demanded to have a pic of Davy and him and the passport. Gyanendra told Davy to get out as soon as possible and leave BC. "I felt like a murderer when the Sherpa attacked me. I thought he was going to throw stones at me." He then left very quickly as he was scared to be hurt physically or arrested. 

He went back to Pumori C1 and BC to pack up his gear and leave. His load was about 32kg. He trekked back to Jiri, which took him 8 days from Pumori BC. He arrived in Kathmandu on 15 May. He went straight to the Department of Tourism on Brikuthi Mandap. He met with the director of the tourism board and he got lectured and they informed him that he was going to be detained. They then called the Chief Police Lady and he was transported to the Nepal Police, where he was held custody for seven days. He went to court for the bail application and was released on bail for 1000 NPR. The decision will be made within two days. The fine is very likely to be around US$24,000U, he can either pay or go to prison. Davy does not have the money so he will go to prison. Maximum sentence 4 years, minimum 2 years.

Davy says:

I am accountable for my actions and I don't expect anyone feeling sorry for me. I did not hurt anyone and I am with piece of the idea that I did not do any harm to anyone. I come from a poor family and my dad, who used to be a building contractor, has no money. 

I trained really hard to be strong. I trained with a hypoxic mask. He was convinced that he would not have needed any help up there. I was going to go to Camp 1, spend 2 days there, then Camp 2, leave my tent there, etc. Ed Viesturs books were great for my preparation. I would like to go back, but I will probably be banned for five years. Next time I come, I will pay for it!

I pleaded with the authorities to do community service, but they would not let me.

The reason for me going up there was to help people. I once got very low in my life and I wanted to help people up there. I put all my savings into two feature films and they did not do as well as I thought they would. So I felt like a failure and that is why I wanted to help up there. 

I used to be a fireman and a rescuer and I thought that if were able to help one single person that would be my summit…much more important than the summit.


AFP, 26 May 2017

Kathmandu (AFP) - Nepal said Friday a South African who tried to climb Mount Everest without a permit can go home without paying a $22,000 fine, but banned him from mountaineering there for 10 years.

Ryan Sean Davy, 43, was found hiding in a cave near Everest base camp nearly two weeks ago after being spotted on the world's highest mountain without a $11,000 climbing permit.

The South African, who has no prior mountaineering experience, said he could not afford the hefty permit fee but wanted to scale Everest and document his experience in a book and film.

"The tourism department has excused me and given me permission to go home," Davy told AFP on Friday.

The head of the tourism department confirmed that Davy had been let off the heavy fine and just given a 10-year ban on climbing mountains in Nepal.

"We have imposed a 10 year ban on him against mountaineering in Nepal. No fine has been imposed," said Dinesh Bhattarai, head of the tourism department.

The department had previously said they were looking at converting the fine to a jail sentence of up to four years if the South African could not pay.

"I've not yet quite processed it but, yeah, I'm ecstatic. I thought they would give me at least two months to a year (in jail). So this is a big surprise. I better leave before they change their mind," Davy said, adding that he was made to hand over the memory cards from his camera.

When caught two weeks ago, the South African told officials he had climbed as far as Camp One -- at 6000 metres (nearly 20,000 feet) -- crossing the treacherous Khumbu icefall, despite not having all the proper equipment.

His passport was confiscated by government officials, who ordered he return to Kathmandu to collect it and pay a fine -- a journey he made by foot because he could not afford a plane ticket.

During questioning in Kathmandu he was arrested under Nepal's strict public order laws for swearing at officials and spent a week in detention.

He was released on a $10 bail on Monday but had to report to the tourism department every day.

Johannesburg-born but US-based, Davy moved to Aspen in Colorado six months ago to prepare for his Everest bid, living out of a van as he was short on cash.

He taught himself to climb by reading mountaineering books and watching YouTube videos.

Davy told AFP that his Everest mission is not yet over and he intended to try to summit from the Tibet side -- this time with a permit.

"I'm going to start raising money for a permit on the Tibet side and finish my climb," he said.

Maybank reached C3 on 18 May. He had carried one oxygen bottle up to C3, which he put on once he got there. He slept on the oxygen and went to C4 on 19 May. When he got to C4 he met a Slovakian, who tried to come into his tent. He had come up from C3 and was on his way to Everest, but decided to go to Lhotse C4 for the night. He stayed with Maybank, who had realized that there was no oxygen for him at C4. Next to his tent there was SST Chinese tent and sent a message down to C2 to tell them that there was no O2 for him at C4.

On 21 May Maybank met Pemba Sherpa, who told him that there was oxygen for him on the crossing between Everest and Lhotse. He went down and brought two bottles up for his summit push. One of them did not work.

On the summit push Maybank left C4 at 11 pm on 21 May. Straightforward climb even though he was very slow. He only used 1-1/2 litres of oxygen and he had been at C4 for two nights. When he reached the summit there were three people just coming down from the summit. He spent about 10 minutes on the summit (he has no summit photo as he only had his phone and it died of battery). He descended to C4, where he arrived at 3 pm. On 23 May down to C2. On 24 May down to BC.

Oxygen: Taken and used from C3 to summit to C2 by Maybank.

The team left C3 on 16 May on their summit push at 12:30 am. From C3 they went straight for the summit. They fixed ropes from 8100m to the summit. There was knee-deep snow on summit day, but apart from that, conditions and weather were good, relatively warm, not too windy. They were protected by the couloirs from the wind. 

The climbing was hard because of the snow and because they had to fix the ropes. O'Brolchain ran out of oxygen early on the way up, but Tshering gave him more. Tshering and Sigurjonsson reached the summit at 4:30 pm, they stayed on the summit for 30 minutes. O'Brolchain reached the summit at 6 pm, spending 10 minutes on the top. 

Sigurjonsson and Tshering descended to C3 where they spent the night. They reached C3 around 9 pm. O'Brolchain spent the night alone in C4, he got there at 11 pm. It was a cold night as he didn't have a sleeping bag and was out of oxygen. On 17 May, Sigurjonsson and Tshering Sherpa went down to BC, O'Brolchain and Mingma (who turned around on summit push at 8300m) went to C2. On 18 May, everyone was back at BC.

ROUTEMEMO:
Kuriki's Plan:

“Hello, Tibetan Base Camp”
Yesterday we got to the Tibetan Base Camp. From here, we head to the Base Camp of The North Wall. Yak carry the luggage.

Now, clouds started to hang over Everest. I hope to get to the base camp of the North Wall without problem.

After arriving at the base camp, we are going to do a ceremony called "Puja" to pray for the safety of this attempt, then I will move to ABC (Advanced Base Camp) of the Normal Route to leave for 7500m to acclimatize to the altitude.

Afterwords, I will go back to the Base Camp of the North Wall to take a rest, then I will move to the Advanced Base Camp of the North Wall, where I start climbing all by myself.

I'm going to share my adventure in full-scale.

This year, my team has small number of people but they are all accustomed to the severe conditions; Two Japanese staff, three Nepalese staff who carry tripods and telephoto lenses, a cook and his assistant.

Before, when I made an attempt from the Nepalese side, I had to bring three groups of teams to share my adventure, because from the Base Camp, they couldn´t see me climbing. I put 2 more teams at Camp 1 and the Base Camp of another mountain called Pu Mori, so we had to be a big group. However last year, I found out that whole the North Wall can be seen from the bottom. This year, I brought only two Japanese staff. They will share my attempt from the Advanced Base Camp, using the telephoto scope.


"Welcome to Everest"

Finally I could have uploaded the movie of the acclimating.

I moved from the base camp of the North Wall to the base camp of the Normal Route, making a half way round Everest. 

Then I planned to climb North Col to acclimating. All the team member couldn't come with me, so now I'm came back and finally could have uploaded this video.

That was a really long way…

I headed the altitude of 7500m, but it was too windy so I turned back at the altitude of 7350m.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6400m due to pulmonary edema and pneumonia

Record: First ascent of the season and first female from Rajasthan. First biggest Indian expedition of civilians. First time 13 climbers from Andhra Pradesh summited.

The rope-fixing team consisting of nine Sherpas: Lhakpa Nuru, Pasang Chhiri, Fura Tshering, Mingma Nuru, Karma Gyaljen, Lhakpa Gyaljen, Tenzing Chhotar, Sanduk Dorje Tamang and Lakpa Nuru of Transcend Adventures Expedition reached the summit between 2 to 3 pm on 11 May which was the first ascent of Spring 2017. The rope-fixing team returned ABC same day. This season Chinese rope-fixing team put up ropes to 8300m and asked expeditions to carry on further rope fixing. The other expedition leaders proposed Transcend Adventures to do the job.

The expedition divided its climbers into three teams. The first team consisting of Thammineni, Sandrana, Kare, Kunja, Vooyaka, Gullamarusu, Pemba Chhepal, Phurba, Tashi Chhumbel, Nurbu, Pasang Dorjee, Phura Tenzing, Kami Temba, Sonam Tashi, Karma Dorje and Lhakpa Tshering Sherpa left C3 at 9 pm (NST) on 12 May for summit. First member arrived to the top at 6:20 am and last one at 7:30 am on 13 May. All members and Sherpas returned C3 before 10 am then continued to descend to ABC last one to arrived ABC at 8 pm. The weather at the beginning 3 to 5 am was windy.

The second team consisting of Gajavelli, Mothukuri, Bodla, Seelam, Phurita, Ngima Tashi, Lhakpa Tenzing, Ngima Tenji, Kami Chhiri and Ang Tendi started their summit push from C3 at 9 pm on 15 May. All got to the top between 4:50 am and 5 am on 16 May. They returned C3 at 10 am and continued to ABC where all arrived between 2 pm and 6 pm.

The third team consisting of Asha Singh, Prabhudesai, Boddu, Vadithe, Ganugapenta, Mingma Nuru, Karma Gyaljen, Lhakpa Gyaljen, Tenzing Chhotar, Sanduk Dorjee Tamang and Pasang Dorjee left C3 between 7:30 to 9 pm on 21 May. First one arrived summit at 3:20 am on 22 May and last one arrived at 4:45 am. They stayed on summit between 10 to 15 minutes. By 11 am all climbers returned C3 and ABC by 8 pm except Ms. Thammineni who spent night at C1 and on 23 May reached ABC. Thammineni went up to 8530m with third team on 22 May and had to turn around due to slowness. 

Leader Bachinepally stayed at BC throughout the expedition. Dr. Patel stayed at ABC.

Ms. Kantepalli climbed up to 7900m. High point for Chinni and Sudipaka was 5700m.

Similarly, high point for Kokkigadda and Madangi was 7100m, and Bagwan was 8000m.

Two members left the expedition earlier who reached mid-camp.

Team left BC on 26 May.

Gilian Lee - 26 Sept 2017

Maximum altitude was Lhotse Camp 4, 7800m on morning of 16 May 2017.  Felt great that morning, but didn't sleep well (I don't use O2).

With me was Ang Dawa Sherpa and Kipa Sherpa. We all turned back to Camp 2 because of massive winds at the summit on the morning of 16 May 2017. We heard from US team that the winds were bad and they were turning around. Dawa recommended that we go down.

We turned back to Camp 2 and rested for a second attempt. Back I could not do a second attempt. Altitude sickness (headache, heavy lungs and fast heart rate) meant that I was not able to get up to Camp 3. Went back to BC on 20 May 2017.

Left BC on 22 May 2017.

Kragh was attempting without oxygen. He aimed to summit on 22 May. He left C3 at 6 am on 22 May. He had already suffered from cold hand and feet the previous day and that's why he left it so late. He was on his own. He reached mushroom rock (8600m) at about 1 pm, but the weather was deteriorating and his extremities were too cold. He descended to C2 (7700m), where he arrived at 6 pm. It was very windy that night. When he woke up the next morning and was putting on his boots at about 5 am, the wind took his tent and he was lifted inside the tent and moved for about 20 meters. It got caught at a rock and stopped. Kragh grabbed the most important belongings and then the tent flew away. He then started his descent to ABC. The first part was still very windy, but then it got better. He reached ABC at about midday.

Pasang Nurbu suffered from a chest infection. He was with van 't Hof, but decided to stay at C3 and not go for the summit. He and van 't Hof descended together after van 't Hof's summit attempt.

Email from Mark van t' Hof - 30 May 2017

My attempt for Everest North was blocked by a sick Sherpa who didn't want to be replaced, although already several weeks a cold and no voice. The sirdar insisted [him] on staying with me.

But in C3 at 8200m, after already days of vomiting and solid coughing, he had to give up. Fell asleep and I had to care for him. And his radio didn't work. So no communication possible between me and Arnold in ABC.

At night, I went to summit without him, together with two pair of members and their Sherpa's.

At nearby Second Step the speed differs between us, I had to go further alone.

According to my GPS I came to 8567m.

  https://connect.garmin.com/modern/activity/1759820652?share_unique_id=57

After thorough competition inside me between ambition and wanted joy of climbing, versus ratio about safety alone in a risky area I decided, with pain in my heart, to return before the Second Step.

No second try was possible in the next days, for several reasons.

I am very disappointed that my try to get to the summit was blocked now for twice, earthquake 2015 and sick, unwilling Sherpa in 2017. No database Everest for me this year.

Went trekking in the Khumbu before flying to Lhasa on 14 April.

On 8 May Marchetti tagged 7500m and returned to ABC and went back to CBC on 10 May. On 14 May, he ascended back to ABC. He took eight hours from C1 to C2 and used one bottle of oxygen. 

On summit push he together with the rest of the Trekking Himalayan team left C3 at 8pm on 20 May. Marchetti carried a heavy pack and Mindu was wondering what he was carrying. They reached mushroom rock at 8500m at 1 am on 21 May. Marchetti said that he was having a big problem with his chest, and said: "Don't worry about the summit Mindu, I will still give you the bonus – just help me get down." They reached 8300m at 2:30 am.

They spent about half an hour resting and having a drink. Frank wanted to go down as quickly as possible. They got to about 30m below the camp. Marchetti got wobbly, could not speak and was unable to communicate. He stared vacantly at the sky and could not sit upright. Mindu turned up the flow rate of his oxygen to 4l per minute. There was no change in his behaviour. He stopped breathing after about 10 minutes. Mindu checked him and attempted CPR for some time, but could not revive him. He stayed with him for about 2 hours and then left.

When Mindu left, Frank was still attached to the fixed line about 30m below high camp.

Judith Raines went only ABC (did not intend to go farther).

Oxygen: Taken and used from C1 to point of death.

The Sherpa deposited the gear at C3 and descended again.

Adamski left C3 at 11 pm on 20 May. The weather was calm. Straightforward climb. He reached the summit with three other people from the north side. He spent about 15 minutes on the summit as it was very cold and windy. 

He descended to C3 on the SOUTH side, where he found an empty tent. He got there at about noon. As he had taken everything including sleeping bag, cooker, gas and food, he had some food and stayed the night at C3.

On 22 May he descended to BC, where he met his friend Andrew, who had brought his trekking boots and clothes. He changed, packed his climbing gear into his bag and went to Gorak Shep, where he spent the night. From Gorak Shep he sent a text message to his friends (Laurent from Switzerland) from the Trekking Himalaya Team and told them that he was Ok and that they should not worry. On 23 May, he received a text message from his wife that there had been an article about him in the Himalayan Times. That's how he found out that he may be in trouble.

On 30 May, he is due to have a meeting at the Ministry of Tourism with the Polish consulate and an embassy official, who has come from Delhi. He is now waiting for his verdict. He is staying because he wants to solve the problem and feels sorry that he got other people into trouble.

Unfortunately, the Chinese authorities announced that due to this illegal traverse Tibet will be closed for ALL mountaineering in autumn 2017.

Oxygen: 
  Taken and used from C3 on north side to top to C4 on south side by Adamski.

ROUTEMEMO:
BC  10/05  4900m
ABC 12/05  6400m
Smt 21/05  by Jornet at midnight (May 21-22)
Smt 27/05  by Jornet at 7 pm.

Acclimatization:

On 13 May from ABC to 7600m in 3-1/2 hours up and one hour down. One rest day. 
On 15 May up to 8400m from ABC 6 hours up, and 3-1/2 hours down. 
On 17 May went down to CBC for 3 days to rest.

First summit push:

On 20 May, Jornet left CBC at 10 pm. Reached ABC at 2:30 am in running shoes. Had a 2 hours to rest and put on mountaineering gear. Started again at 4:30 am. Got to C1 at 6:10 am on 21 May. Reached C3 at 11:30 am. He did not use the fixed ropes and climbed the first two ladders on the Second Step. He used the last ladder on the Second Step.

He reached the summit at midnight. Spent about 10 minutes on the summit. It was clear and not so cold. On the summit, he saw all the headlamps coming up, but no view in the dark. 

From the bottom of the Third Step, he was passing people going up. He was suffering from an upset stomach, which gave him more trouble than the high altitude. He reached ABC at 6 am.

He started to feel better at ABC. Monica Piris, the doctor for Alpenglow Expeditions, gave him some medication. On 23 May he descended to CBC, where he recovered. On 25 May he went back to ABC.

He had carried his rucksack to C3 and left it there on the last leg to the summit. He changed into his downsuit at 7700m. Jornet wears a Salomon boot, which allows him to wear his running shoes inside.

From CBC to summit to ABC: 26 hours ascent; round trip 33 hours.

Second summit push:

On 27 May, he left ABC at 2 am. The reason why he went quite late was the weather forecast, which had predicted high winds for later in the day. Reached C1 at 4 am. He did not make a note for C2. C3 at 10 am. Summit at 7 pm. The weather was windy and snowy. He spent about 10 minutes in the summit. 

On the way down to C3, he picked up his rucksack and continued for a bit, but then had a blackout. The next memory he has is crossing over toward the North Face. He did not know how he got there. It was around midnight and he had the urgency to sleep. At about 8000m he got under a rock in the rocky part around in the Norton Couloir. He does not remember when left the North Ridge. The only memory he has is climbing toward the right. After having slept for about 2 hours, he woke up at 2 am, his brain was working again. He looked at the GPS and crossed back toward the North Ridge. He thinks he went across the Messner Crossing to get back to the North Ridge again.

After sleeping for about 2 hours. And on 28 May he reached ABC at 6 am.

From ABC to summit: 17 hours; round trip back to ABC 26 hours.

Left ABC: 28 May
Left CBC: 29 May

Oxygen: Not taken, not used.

On the way up Anita lost her mittens at C3, she had to summit with normal gloves. She felt cold at her hands, but luckily didn't get any frostbite. Tarke's oxygen mask broke down at the 2nd Step. After one hour he got a new mask from another Sherpa.

Anita Devi is the first Indian woman to summit Everest from both North and South sides.

Climb not difficult, BC to C1 easy but long, C1 to C2 difficult but ropes made it easy.

Started at 4 am from C2. Reached summit at 12 pm. Stayed on top for 30 minutes. Weather was good until 10 am then deteriorated. After summit team went down to C2, wanted to go down to C1 initially, but found a sick Korean climber at 6600m. He was alone when they found him.
 
He was in a team of 3 Koreans and 2 Sherpas, all Koreans got sick. Sherpas could only bring down 2 Koreans, so one of them had to stay behind alone. Sherpas had given him oxygen, but he showed no reaction. Arias gave him an injection of dexamethasone. Sherpas had called their BC for rescue team, BC promised to send Sherpas, but Sherpas never came. 

Meanwhile, Ramon Pont continued descent, he arrived at C2 at maybe 8 pm. Arias and Aymar stayed with the Korean, they tried to talk to him, but he was unable to speak. They tried to bring him down using their rope, but it was too difficult. At 6 pm, Spaniards built a snow cave for the Korean. They had to descend themselves as it was getting dark. It was windy and snowing. They put the Korean in the snow cave and secured him using ice screws. Korean tried to speak, but Spaniards couldn't understand him. Then they went down, they descended maybe 10 meters when the Korean started to scream. They looked up and saw him removing the anchors, one by one. Removing the last anchor, he fell down West Face, for maybe 1500m. Spaniards saw him falling down, maybe 10 meters away. He fell from 6580m at 7:30 pm. His name / team / agency is unknown to Spaniards, Arias estimated him to be 65 years old, he was wearing a black/yellow downsuit. 

Arias and Aymar arrived at C2 at 11 pm. On 2 Nov all three members went down to BC. Body of Korean was found and flown out by helicopter two days after accident. 

Team left BC on 3 Nov.

Oxygen: Not taken not used.

On the descent from summit they saw a German climber (~55 years old, name unknown) who had fallen and looked quite bad, possibly suffering from AMS as well. He was together with his Sherpa, who looked tired as well. They didn't talk much, but stayed with them for maybe 1-2 hours. The Sherpa eventually called per satellite phone two other Sherpas to come up and help the German client down. After that phone call, Spaniards continued their descent to C2. They saw the German climber again a few days later at BC, they didn't talk to him, but he looked alright. Team was back at BC on 26 Oct at 6 pm.
  
No support staff above BC, they carried everything themselves.

Team left BC on 30 Oct.

ROUTEMEMO:
From Htoo Group Media department:

20 Nov 2017

The mountaineering team had arrived at the Base-Camp of Mount Ama Dablam, at the date of November 18th, 2017. The Ama Dablam’s height and hardship are approximately same with the Khakaborazi, which is the highest glacier mountain of Myanmar.

They had ascended the Camp-1 on November 19th, 2017 and tomorrow, they are to ascend and achieve the Camp-3. Moreover, it is informed that the Myanmar Mountaineering Team will not take rest at the Camp-3 but they will continue their expedition to successfully reach the peak of the mountain via Camp-3 to summit vice versa summit to Camp-2 at the date of November 21st, 2017. According to their schedule, on November 22nd, 2017, they are to descend Base Camp from Camp-2. On November 23rd, 2017, they are to get Pangboche Village, where they have started their expedition.

Three Myanmar mountaineers from Myanmar Hiking and Mountaineering Federation, Ko Pyae Phyo Aung, Ko Aung Khaing Myint and Ko Zaw Zin Khaing, who will make tremendous efforts to conquer the Mount Khakaborazi in Myanmar, and are mainly sponsored by the Htoo Group of Companies and Htoo Foundation, left the Yangon International Airport for Nepal to make mountaineering practices at the Ama Dablam Mountain, 6812 m above sea level, in Nepal, at 7:45 pm, October 23rd, 2017.

23 Nov 2017

Three members of Myanmar Hiking and Mountaineering Association, Ko Pyae Phyo Aung, Ko Zaw Zin Khaing and Ko Aung Khaing Myint have started the journey on 10.05 hours in the morning and could set foot on the peak of Ama Dablam, which is 6812 meters high. After that, they have descended to Camp-2 by 7:00 hours in the evening.

From Camp-2, three Myanmar climbers have descended to the base camp in Pangboche by 3:45 hours in the evening.

ROUTEMEMO:
Tom Boydston - 17 Jan 2018

Starting as a team of 6 from High Camp (17,850) Darryl Sargent, Lhakpa Sherpa and I (Tom Boydston) summited Chulu West (21,062 ft or 6420m) October 23, 2017 in total white-out conditions returning back to High Camp in near white-out conditions which persisted through the descent. Total time was approximately 14 hours - HC to HC.

After our successful Chulu West summit, we moved from Chulu West base camp and set up our Annapurna IV base camp October 27-29, 2017 in a flat area commonly used for summit attempts of Annapurna IV and II. Our climbing plan and style was to establish 3 high camps up the NW buttress of Annapurna IV. Due to a severe respiratory cough on my part, I was only able to make it up to Camp 1, whereas Darryl Sargent, Lhakpa Sherpa and Ngima Tenzing Sherpa were able to carry and establish up to Camp 2 at approximately 19,400 ft. The team of 3 (Darryl, Lhakpa, and Ngima) were turned back by a combination of extreme cold temperatures, high wind, and snow as well as high uncertainty about the route above Camp 2 up to Camp 3. The route to Camp 3 has substantially changed, becoming more difficult, apparently due to the massive 2015 earthquake in Nepal, which exposed large (100m or more) glacial ice seracs guarding the possible routes to the dome of Camp 3.

Arriving at BC we had 2-6 inches of snow. Over the next 2 weeks, we experienced generally clear weather moderate winds, but falling temperatures. The route to C1 was notably devoid of snow requiring 3rd to 5th class (YDS) rock climbing. The route to C2 was hard snow over glacial ice, to bare ice.

Abandoned expedition due to extreme cold, snow and wind, and uncertainty about route to from C2 to C3 due to new large ice seracs above C2 due to 2015 earthquake.

Fixed rope: approximately 1000m various sections up to C2.

Oxygen: Not taken, not used.
ACCIDENTS: Boydston evacuated by helicopter on 8 Nov due to severe respiratory cough

McDonald and Lewis wanted an early summit push. They ascended to C2 on 2 Nov. Lewis stayed at C2. It was too cold him, he was handicapped by previous cold injury damage at his toes.

McDonald, Phurba and Sange started from C2 on 3 Nov at 1 am. They summitted at 11 am. There was a lot of snow, thigh-deep, about 30-40cm of new snow at C2. Phurba had fixed ropes up to the summit together with the Summitclimb Sherpas the day before. They summitted in clouds, no visibility and had to descend through snowfall. They arrived at C2 at 5 pm. McDonald was extremely exhausted. On 4 Nov they went down to BC.

Dave Pritt and Nicholas Hateley slept at C1 for a night, then went down again. They had heard conditions were very bad on the mountain and didn't want to go any higher.

Niall Connolly reached C1 on 2 Nov; he had a chest infection and had to abandon climb.

It was cold every day, most days it clouded over during the expedition. 

Persson reached her high point at C1 on 2 Nov. On 3 Nov she and Connolly were brought down to BC by Noble. Persson felt tired and exhausted, weather forecast was bad, prohibiting a summit attempt. On the way out between Baruntse BC and Amphu Laptsa Pass Persson's condition worsened. She had been coughing a lot and had been slow. She realized she couldn't do the pass in her condition and decided to have a helicopter evacuation the next day. During night at Amphu Laptsa BC she was given oxygen. On 8 Nov she and Wangda Sherpa were evacuated from Amphu Laptsa BC to Ktm via Lukla. She was hospitalized in Ktm, and diagnosed with AMS by doctor. She believes she was suffering from a chest infection rather than AMS. She's slowly getting better now.

Noble, Connolly and Lewis crossed the Amphu Laptsa on the way out and flew to Ktm from Lukla.

Favero and Kalden Phura started for summit from C2 at 2:15 am. They turned around at 9:30 am; they were fast, but last part to summit was very dangerous to negotiate in the bad snow conditions. It had been hard climbing up the slope to 7000m through a lot of snow, no tracks; they had to break trail and were the only ones going for the summit. Old tracks were not there anymore, fixed ropes were buried by snow. 

They descended to BC the same day, arriving at 5 pm.  It was very cold, -28° Celsius near summit, -20° Celsius at C2.

Favero initially wanted to go for the summit from C1 earlier when conditions were better, but her Sherpa objected as he thought it would be too long and too hard from C1. So they had to establish C2 first and when they were ready for summit push, conditions got worse. A lot of snowdrifting.

Moretto didn't feel well, couldn't handle altitude and never got higher than BC. He went to Tagnag and waited for Favero.

Team left BC on 7 Nov trekking via Mera La to Lukla.

ROUTEMEMO:
Approach: Flight to Johmson, trek to Muktinath, Green Camp, Narsing Khola, Kog, Kampa's Headquarter Camp, Bhrikuti BC, Khumjumgar BC.

HPt:  01/11  6269m by Swan, Fornerod, Temba Bhote.

Climb abandoned on 1 Nov due to coldness.

They had an intense approach, very difficult approach march. Between Khumjumgar BC and Dhey La Camp they set up a camp at 5873m on 31 Oct. The plan was next day to have some people to climb Bhrikuti while porters and other members would carry loads and go over unnamed pass at 6300m west of Bhrikuti. They eventually wanted to cross the Saribung Pass and walk into Nar Phu.

Summit group (Swan, Fornerod, Temba Bhote) left camp at 5:30 am on 01 Nov. Long walk on glacier with big crevasses. They took a wrong way, ending up at max altitude at 6269m at pass NW of Bhrikuti. It was 8:30 am. They would have had time to go to summit, but it was extremely cold. They estimated it to be -40 to -50° Celsius. They decided not to climb it. Going down they had to abseil, very steep ice slope, much more difficult than going up.

The others at camp had started to go up already, when Swan radioed them and told them that they would go back the way they came. It would have been too dangerous for the whole team to cross the pass and continue to Saribung Pass in the cold conditions. The whole team went down to a place a little further down than Khumjumgar BC near bottom of glacier at 5399m. They arrived at camp at 3:20 pm that day.

Porters were not well enough equipped for such an extreme mountain trip in those conditions according to Swan. Lots of crevasses, a long way, very cold. A lighter team would have been needed to go over pass and traverse into Nar Phu.

Next day on 2 Nov team went down to Kampa's HQ camp. Then to Tangge. On 4 Nov to Tangge Pa. On 5 Nov from Pa to Chuksang. From there Jeep to Jomson, and flight to Pokhara.

ROUTEMEMO:
BC  27/09  5300m

Smt 05/10  by Hanna, Naga Dorje, Sonam Samden, Pemba Tshering at 12:05 pm.

From base camp at 5300m, the team crossed the glacier without a rope as it was mainly moraine. Once on the base of the mountain, they scrambled up a small couloir. On the top of the couloir they established crampon point at about 5700m. From there they went up the snow face to gain the ridge, where they established C1 at 6200m. From C1 to C2 they climbed up the SE Ridge. The ridge was about 50 degrees. At 6200m they came across a few crevasses, which they could negotiate via snow bridges. 

At about 6400m, the Sherpas did not want to carry on due to the crevasses, but Hanna pushed on. The Sherpas put him on belay and he fixed rope and took them across. They established C2 at 6600m right next to a big crevasse on the flat.

On summit day, the summit team left C2 at 6 am. They had to cross the big crevasse and climbed the headwall with the Sherpas fixing the ropes. The headwall was about 70 degrees. The snow on the headwall was sugary and they had to break trails. Snow was up to their knees. Hanna left some ashes of his dog on the summit and they drank a bottle of Moet Champagne. After they spent about half an hour on the summit, they descended to BC, where they arrived at about 6:10 pm.

Kershner reached C2 on 4 Oct. He was suffering from altitude sickeness and decided not to come on the summit push. He stayed at C2 and waited for the summit team to come back.

Burke reached 6300m on 5 Oct. He was on his way to C2 to join the rest of the team for a summit attempt. However, as the weather forecast was bad for the following days, Hanna decided to push for the summit. When Burke found out that Hanna and the Sherpas reached the summit, it was clear to him that he would not carry on. It was important to him that someone of the team reached the summit.

Team left BC by heli to Lukla on 7 Oct, and on to Ktm the same day.

ROUTEMEMO:
BC  25/10  4500m (between Beding and Chekigo)

Smt 27/10  by Pablo Ruiz, Jesus Ibarz, Omar Juan via W Face at 4 pm (1st ascent)

Spanish talked about West Summit of Chekigo at 6121m (according to Finn Map). Pt 6121 not crossed by 1st ascent team in 2005 who climbed Chekigo via West Face as well (CHEK-051-01).

Spaniards did two ascents to Pt 6121, west of Chekigo, one via West Face and one via SE Face. All in alpine style. Plan had always been that subpeak, they never attempted main summit of Chekigo.

West Face team consisted of Ruiz, Ibarz and Juan. They started from their camp at the foot of the face at 2 am and got to the top of Pt 6121 at 4 pm. Entire face is 900m. Some very steep climbing (80°), some mixed climbing MD+ (Spanish scale). Crux sections at mixed terrain at middle of wall and near the summit. Very good conditions, ice in very good shape, it wasn't too cold. They stayed on the top for maybe 15 minutes. Descent via same route, rappelled down the entire face. Back at ABC at 10 pm. 

Zabalza and his wife Sonia Casas tried to climb SE Face from Na village on 4 Nov, but Casas had problems with altitude and only reached 5500m. Both descended together to Na.

On 7 Nov Zabalza and Ibarz tried SE Face again. They had one camp after Na at 5150m at base of SE Face and slept there on 6 Nov. They left camp at 4 am, reached the summit at 1 pm and were back at camp at 5:30 pm. Conditions were good, not perfect. The summit was all in clouds, they had no visibility from the top. Climbing maybe V at the rocks, some parts as steep as 70-80°. SE Face not as difficult as West Face. Descent via same route as ascent, they rappelled a total of 15 pitches.  

Madoz didn't climb because of personal problems.
 
Valle and Larranaga couldn't deal with altitude (they only climbed Yalung Ri).

Team left BC on 10 Nov.

Steep ice, 60-85°. Very exposed summit ridge, cornices, sugar snow conditions.

Team left BC on 25 Oct to Ama Dablam.

Smt 21/10  by all members and Sherpas and 12:10 pm.

After having attempted the West Ridge (see below), the team decided to do the normal route on the N ridge. On 21 Oct at 3:32 am they started from BC. They moved on the rope together. The gradient was about 30 to 35 degrees. Conditions were good at the beginning, but farther up there was a hard cover. It was windy, but clear. They spent about 20 minutes on the summit and descended to BC, where they arrived at 5:10 pm.

West Ridge attempt:

HPt  19/10  5800m  by Guenzel, Schuetz, Dawa Gyalje and Pasang Gomba at 1:20 pm.

Team attempted the West Ridge to open a new route. They climbed across the moraine towards the west until their reached a rocky tower to gain the ridge, which was covered in firn snow, where they roped up. They negotiated a steep flank of around 60 degrees, which was followed by another sharp ridge. The ridge ended up in a broader slope, which was covered in a few crevasses and was undulating and not steeper than about 45 degrees. Snow was about shin-deep and some of the crevasses they had to circumvent towards the east.

When they reached the high point they realised that it would too cumbersome to carry on as the ridge was only about 2 fingers wide. They would not have had enough time and the capacities not continue.

Moeller reached to about 5700m, but decided not to continue with the others as he thought it was too risky for him. 

On their way down they picked up Moeller and descended to BC, where they arrived at 5:45 pm. 

Team left BC on 23 Oct; arrived Kathmandu 25 Oct by vehicle from Manang.

Paulo Grobel - American Alpine Journal (online)

Despite the long march, it was a privilege to visit the last village before the mountain, Ku. From there to our base camp at 5040m, which we reached on October 3, it was a great wilderness experience.

We established advanced base at 5750m on the 4th and then climbed to the summit and back to base the following day. Climbing in five separate pairs, and reaching the top, were Laurence De Fleurian and Dhan Magar, Bernard Vallet and Etienne De Fleurian, Jean-François Males and Etienne Principaud, Anil Rai and Deepen Bothe, and Sonia Baillif and Paulo Grobel.

High point team started from C3 at 10 pm on 14 Sept. At first they took the wrong coloir to about 7900m. The Sherpas radioed down to the kitchen and contacted Chang Dawa of Seven Summit and noticed that they were in the wrong coloir. They decided to traverse farther to the right looking for the right coloir. However, they were not sure where the right coloir was and decided to descend at about midday.  

They descended to C3 where they arrived at 7 pm. On 16 Sept the members descended to C1 to stay the night as they did not want to cross the Eiger zone late in the afternoon. The Sherpas continued to BC.

On 17 Sept everyone at BC.

They stayed at BC for one week. The weather was bad and on 26 Sept they tried again to ascend, but only reached C1. When they reached there, their tents were destroyed. They recovered the material and descended to BC. The conditions were too dangerous. Pasang Nurbu and Ngima Dorchi stayed the night at C1 and ascended to C3 the following day to fetch oxygen regulators.

Cacavilla left the expedition on 25 Sept. Rest left BC 29 Sept in helicopter to Kathmandu.

Summit day: Started at 1:45 am from C3, made all tracks alone from 7500m to the summit (climb rate: 11:30 for 900m => 78mh).

From exit on the summit ridge to the summit is 300m long, 10 minutes easy walk. 
Summited at 1:10 pm. Put a snow bar on top with bamboo stick. Kruglov later did picture with it. Returned on same day to at C3 at 6:30 pm. Petrov left BC on 1 Oct.

Petrov used the exit couloir further right/west of the usual one, the latter seemingly to be in better conditions in spring (without cornices) according to him.

Yuri Kruglov summited the day after at 9 am (started at 9 pm on 1 Oct) with 2 Sherpas (Himalayan Guides). 

On 15 Sept with Carlos Soria, Boyan reached the eastern ridge at a point down to the foresumit (c8055m), as Pemba Sherpa first told him to go there. As it was in the fog with dubious snow conditions on the ridge, he choose not to push farther.  

Luis Carcavilla (geologist of Madrid) left BC 23 Sept. On 15 Sept, he went with Soria up to the footstep of the spur right to the usual exit couloir (made a deposit there at c7900m). Spur above it sizes c150m high.

              Dendi Sherpa at 8:30 am.

Started at 9 pm from C3 the day before, climbing up fixing ropes (impossible de déterrer cordes of Soria sous un m de neige, packed snow, at about 7800m, left these and traversed by themselves simultaneously – because night time, not knowing where to go…). Then traversed to the left towards the exit couloir. Broke trails, each of them, every 20m changing (why used ox). Snow sometimes to the hips for the tracks on the large traverse (drum sound, then collapsed). 

30 minutes after departure from C3 at 7500m Benjamin Kane (with YK on MANA last 2016) wanted to climb quicker behind them. When they reached 7500m, Kane's brand new Petzl headlamp failed (spare batteries, replaced, not working). Radio light, not working. No proper light, Kane could go no farther. Couldn't wait (-17°C) in the night, so went back to C3 that day. Was really prepared for the top that day.   
Summit for Kruglov and Ang Phurba at 8:30 am. Lots of time on summit. Back at BC at 9 pm.

Knew great forecast, knew peaceful weather until midday. So had time. Also didn't want to leave Dendi alone. Episod / Italians on 20 Sept!

Dendi Sherpa was behind them. Started with them. Either sick or weak? 300m behind...they crossed each other at the corridor exit when Kruglov was going down. Dendi Sherpa summited (picture by Kruglov) at about 9:30-10 am.

Team left BC on 3 Oct.

Fixed rope: put a short rope below the summit ridge (in rucksack, climb solo, then anchor and abseil left there); above C3, used fixed ropes left by Carlos team (bad quality-anchors shitty; just clipped carabiner on the fixed rope on descent, but didn't hold it) (too steep, too far left).

Smt 08/10  by Cimenti at 2.15 pm, Koenig at 3 pm.

The summit team had intended to start at 10:30 pm on 7 Oct, but it was too windy and Koenig was not feeling well. At about 12:30 am Cimenti left for the summit while Koenig stayed in the tent as his upset stomach was still bothering him. At about 3 am, Koenig felt better and left the tent at about 3:30 am to follow Cimenti.

The snow was about half a meter deep and Cimenti had to break trail. He sometimes broke through the crust and it was hard work. There was some fixed rope on the traverse from about 7600m to 7890m which was probably fixed by Ang Phurba Sherpa of the Russian/Australian expedition. He traversed toward the last couloir that leads up to the ridge. The couloir is about 150m long, which was also fixed. Couloir was snowy. Once Cimenti got to the top of the couloir it took him about 20 minutes to get to the summit, where he spent about 35 minutes. It was still windy, but very clear. When he descended, he crossed Koenig, who was on his way up, just on the exit of the couloirs. Koenig spent about 15 minutes on the summit. On his descent he caught up with Cimenti and they both descended to C3, where they arrived between 6:30 pm (Koenig) and 7 pm (Cimenti).

The following day they descended to BC. On this final summit push, they did not take their skis with them. They had tried twice with skies, but when it came to the final attempt, they decided that they would just concentrate on the climbing rather than on the skiing.

Gerlero reached 7900m on the second summit push on 29 Sept. He did not feel well and turned back. On the final summit push, he reached C2. He felt unwell and descended to BC at midnight.

Skiing: 

On the second summit push on 29 Sept, Gerlero and Cimenti took their skis to the beginning of the traverse at about 7600m. They deposited their skis there and continued to about 7900m (Gerlero) and 8000m (Cimenti). Cimenti was following Boyan Petrov, but when Petrov decided not to take the first couloir and continued to the traverse, Cimenti decided that he would not have the energy to carry on and turned back.

When Cimenti and Gerlero turned back they picked up their skis and carried them down to C3 as it felt more comfortable than skiing. They spent another night at C3 and on 30 Sept, Cimenti and Gerlero carried their skis to half way to C2 (about 6900m) and skied all the way down past the Little Eiger to about 5000m.

On the second summit push on 28 Sept, Koenig left his skis at 7400m and continued to about 7500m, but was too sick to continue with Gerlero and Cimenti. On his descent, he carried his skis to C3 and stayed the night there. On 29 Sept, he skied all the way from C3 to the end of the glacier near the Little Eiger to below C1 at about 5000m.

Team left BC 10 Oct.

OTHERSMTS: Climbed 5840m unnamed peak near Dolma Khang
ROUTEMEMO:
Note: team initially thought that they had climbed Dolma Khang (6332m), but later determined that they climbed a nearby 5840m unnamed subpeak.


BC  31/10  4750m located on rocks at south site of mountain right under the S Face
Smt 05/11  by Rasmussen, Dawa Tashi, Thundu, Tamting at 10:10 am.
	  
Left BC on 6 Nov by helicopter from BC to Ktmdu.

Route: ascent on SW Face, maybe last bit on [North]West Ridge (Rasmussen not sure). Descent via same route. 

Mountain lies on Tibetan border, just SE of Gaurishankar, between Gaurishankar and Beding Go.

Rasmussen wanted to climb Ramdung initially, then he found out that it has been climbed already and Lakpa from Ascent Himalayas came up with Dolma Khang as another option.

Total expedition consisted of Rasmussen, three climbing Sherpas, one cook, two cook assistants. Sherpas had spent 18 days locating the mountain and preparing BC before Rasmussen flew in by helicopter. Rasmussen had no prior acclimatization and suffered from strong headache first few days at BC.
 
On summit day he and 3 Sherpas started from BC at 2:30 am. They went straight for the summit from BC, no high camps. Some sections were tricky, scree slopes with a lot of lose rocks. Some climbing as steep as 80-degrees, maybe 4+ according to him. A lot of rockfall, Rasmussen was hit by small rocks on helmet and arms, got some bruises. Section reminded him of his climb on Ama Dablam, but more dangerous. After steep part they reached a ridge, very narrow ridge, Rasmussen more or less crawled along ridge because it was so exposed, Sherpas walked normally. Didn't follow whole ridge to summit, but traversed slope a few meters below summit. From their location they weren't sure which exit led to the true summit, it took some time until they found it. Highest point quite narrow, maximum of two persons could stand there at the same time. At 10:10 am they stood on the top. Took abseils on descent at steep part. At maybe 5100m between crampon point and BC Tamting took a fall on scree slope, rolled down the slope for some meters before he stopped. By falling he had released some rocks, one hit him at hip. Hip is okay now, but he may have broken of his fingers during fall; he's undergoing treatment in Kathmandu now. At 2:30 pm everyone was back at BC. Rasmussen had strong headache after the climb and used oxygen during the night.

Dolma Khang is also known as Tseringma and it has been climbed by Australian expedition in 1980 trying the SE Ridge of Gaurishankar (GAUR-801-01).

Oxygen: 
  Taken and used by Rasmussen intermittently from maybe 5800m to summit and on 
  descent. At BC during night after summit.

Txikon thinks that compared to last year, the expedition was very similar. One of the biggest differences, however, was the fact that even though they were only six climbers and not 11 like in 2017, they only took 9 days to fix the rope up to the Yellow Band. Last year, it took them 20 days.

The icefall doctors (Tenzing and Gyalzen) only went as far as C2.

Nuri, Ali Sadpara and Temba Bhote were focused on fixing the rope up to C2 (6100m) until 20 Jan (when they summited Pumori), which means before that, only six people were working and fixing the rope on Everest.

For their second rotation, they left BC on 27 Jan, reached C2 on 28 Jan and C3 on 29 Jan. On 30/01 they set out in the morning to reach C4. Pasang Sherpa and Walung had set out a little bit earlier than the rest of the team. When they all reached their high points, they turned back due to the cold and the high winds. According to Messner, they were not attempting Tarke Kang (aka Glacier Dome), but Tharke Kang, which is east of it and is 7140m. [This peak is also known as Gangapurna West, Asapurna, or Lachenal Peak and is on the Ministry list of open peaks. This appears to be the first attempt from the north side.]

Upon arrival weather was good, however, there was a lot of snow. Trail breaking up to the knees, sometimes hips. Route itself not too technically difficult. About 70 degrees. The first time they reached 6000m they pitched a tent and stayed the night. However, before they set up their tent an avalanche came down at about 5:30 pm and took both climbers with it. Pruenster was in the avalanche for about 10 metres and nearly got swept down the abyss, but stopped before.

They pitched tent on the debris at 6000m and stayed the night. The next day they descended to BC. Bad weather period followed. A few days later they climbed back for another acclimatization rotation. They pitched their tent a bit higher at 6200m and it snowed a lot during the night and everything got wet. They descended the following day. Complete whiteout, but they made it back to BC. The following three days snowfall.

On 22 Sept, Messner climbed back up in very good conditions with the new snow being frozen. This time, Pruenster did not go up with him due to stomach problems. Messner was intending to head for the summit due to the excellent conditions; however, when he reached the spot where the tent was, it was gone. Probably swept away by an avalanche. He dug for two hours, but could not find anything. He descended back to BC.

Climb abandoned due to missing material. They were both very lucky as they were not in the tent when avalanche came down.

Team left BC on 23 Sept and returned to Ktmdu on 29 Sept by trekking back to Manang and then Besisahar.

ROUTEMEMO:
BC  14/10  4900m at "new Himlung BC" opposite of "old Himlung BC", on southern 
                 side of Pangri-Glacier
