ROUTEMEMO:
Summit of Limi Koti:            Oct 5 by Lestienne group
Summit of Shikan Ri:            Oct 9 by Damilano group
Attempt of Peak without Name 1: Oct 11 by Lestienne group plus FD and UL
Summit of Peak without Name 2 : Oct 11 by Lestienne group plus FD and UL
Summit of Pasang Peak:          Oct 14 by Lestienne group
West summit of Pasang Peak:     Oct 14 by UL and FD

2 French teams combining Damilano group and Lestienne groups took permit for Aichyn, but climbed other peaks. Made round trip from Simikot around Limi Himal. Group of Lestienne included Lestienne, Florian Pugin, Julien Desecures and Evelyne Miot. The other French group following slightly different schedule was led by Francois Damilano with Ulysse Lefebvre and 2 clients (Perazio and Talopp). Joined all together at BC.

Schedule : 
  Arrival date at Simikot: Sept 28
  Trekking starting date:  Sept 29

Lestienne and Damilano's group walked together via villages including the one of Hepka, then at Selina Camp and Talung camps. From there Damilano and Lestienne group split; the latter walked up eastwards aiming for "Limi Koti" (a peak without name 6190m/Alti 6170m; christening proposal of "Limi Koti" for it).

Oct 4: established BC at 4960m, west from the Limit Koti.

Oct 5: climbed to the top via SW Face, then SW Ridge. Average difficulties estimated to (800m/AD-/snow up to 50°) in French grade. First intended to traverse eastward then northeast to reach Nying BC, but without much acclimatization and a weather forecast not annouced good enough, so turned back from summit back to BC on that day.

Lestienne group couldn't get mules from Damilano despite messages sent (Damilano never got it) so would have to carry their gear by themselves. Lestienne group walked after Damilano via Nying Khola (W to E orientated) flat dry valley, 11 kms long, magnificent place with yacks spread everywhere and sheperds huts.
 
Oct 7: Lestienne group slept at Bishal BC at 4800m.
Oct 8: Lestienne group reached Nying BC, where Damilano group had already arrived 
       on Oct 6.

Meanwhile Damilano group had been up for reconnaissance on Oct 6 to C1 site located at 5270m; Damilano group went back to C1 for deposit on Oct 7 at 5270m then back to BC; Damilano group slept at C1 on Oct 8; Damilano group summited Shikan Ri (5996m) on Oct 9 (700m/F), clients going back to BC on that day when FD and UL stayed at C1.

Oct  9: Lestienne group had a rest day after intense walking days.
Oct 10: Lestienne group went up to sleep at C1 where FD and UL were staying.

Oct 11: all 6 members (Damilano, Lefebvre, Lestienne, Desecures, Miot, Pugin) attempted Peak Without Name 1, kind of Dent du Geant (58XXm) via its N Face (very difficult route, TD equivalent), about 100m high and located east of Shikan Ri. Team met with plaquee snow, so stopped 40m below the summit due to delicate mixed ground conditions. Then they climbed the good looking little Peak Without Name 2 located between PWN 1 and Shikan Ri (5996m). Storm came at beginning of the descent at about 5800m, losing trail from there. 3 mountain guides but had no GPS tracking! Lestienne was testing the ground in front, and hardly seeing, eventually they could find route back down. At C1 got even harder conditions. In the windy snowy night got up to 30cms of snow fall.

Oct 12: all to BC.
Oct 13: all had rest day.

Oct 14: Lestienne group without FD and UL set up a new C1 eastwards from Nying BC at 5300m below Pasang peak (altitude 6065/6070 GPS 6061 +-5m). Meanwhile, UL and FD were setting an other C1 NNEastwards from Nying BC at c.5200m.

Oct 15: Lestienne group climbed Phasang peak from south face towards a couloir leading up to 40m below the summit. Met with rotten rock from there to the top (average difficulties of PD+/AD- grade, 45° slopes maximum). On that day Floriane and Grégoire went down to Nying BC ; Julien and Evelyne slept at C1.

Meanwhile FD and UL had climbed the W Face of the Western foresummit of Pasang Peak. Went back down straight to BC on that day.

Oct 16: east of BC, established a new multi-pitch route on the right side of a granite rock face, called "Tour MCL" (exit at 5200m, start at c.5000m): "Li Mini Keums" (250m/4c-5a), climbing with big shoes. Nice rock, possibilities on steeper part of the face to the left. Other people were resting. Julien and Evelyne were back to BC for resting on that day.

Oct 17: all 8 members left BC with muleteers aiming to reach Pasang La (5448m, north-eastwards from Nying BC) despite trying it out of the official opening period (June to September on the maps, since mules cannot reach the col in snowy conditions). Despite deteriorating weather coming from SW, they made it and slept at Tsutuma on that evening.

Lestienne group initially intended to traverse Limi massif first W-E traverse to Nying BC, then N-S towards Simikot. Lack of acclimatization at the beginning, weather conditions and mountain conditions (snowy northern parts were generally offering deep snow to break the trail..."we would have needed rackets!") prevented them to try both times.

Oct 20: reached Simikot.

Potential for climbing:

Nying BC already got 6/7 BC areas set up there => previous teams went there for sure.

- Area explored by Freeman-Atwood (comes here since 10 years, has his gear stored at Simikot); southeast part of the massif = Dojan Himal (6246m), tried for the least (?) by Julian Freeman-Atwood.

- Japanese came last year: Sumdo Himal christiened by them?

Nying Ri and Nying are both magnificent summits with lots of lines possibilities. Paulo Grobel doesn't think it's climbed.

Pasang likely was likely already climbed.

Rocky face east from Nying BC offering opening possibilities on the steeper left side plus a nice pillar above it to link.

Massive face of Gurka Khang east of Tangjung (Freeman-Atwood?).

Massive rocky valley potential between Dojan (1 day from Simikot) and Tangjung villages, with kind of Les Drus tower on the east side of it. Kind of accessible Taghia valley!

Laura Mares, legitimized the Alpine Club Carpathian Bucharest Division Elite Mountaineering, accompanied by Justin Ionescu, coach mountaineering and climbing, managed on 22 October at 10 am, the ascent to the highest point of Ama Dablam (6812m), without Sherpa support and without the use of bottled oxygen, according to the Romanian Federation of climbing and climbing.

The expedition began on October 5 in Kathmandu, the two following a necessary acclimatization Valley Kumbu to Ama Dablam base camp and several mountain tours, during which they arranged themselves two intermediate camps to the top.

The rise began in Camp 2, at 2 am and lasted 8:00, and covers a route very technical and steep overhangs in places involving mixed climbing on rock, ice and snow. Its sharp edges and steep faces make Ama Dablam be called the Matterhorn of the Himalayas.

From the Himalayan Times, 16 Nov 2018

An Australian climber was killed when a huge rock fell from the high camp of Mt Ama Dablam damaging the climbing route in the Mt Everest region, according to the expedition organiser.

Tshering Pande Bhote, Managing Director at Top Himalaya Guides Pvt Ltd said that 33-year-old Michael Geoffrey Davis from Australia fell to his death above Camp II on Mt Ama Dablam after a huge rock damaged the climbing ropes that the climbers were using for descend the 6812 metre-peak yesterday.

According to Bhote, the incident occurred when a group of 15 climbers including high altitude guides were descending from Camp III [site] following a strong wind. “The mountaineer fell to his death after a huge rock damaged the ropes he was using to descend the mountain,” he informed. Other members of the expedition were, however, safe, he added.

AMA DABLAM - ANGELO IN CIMA

Nepal, Ama Dablam (6812). Today October 27, 2018 Angelo Lobina has trodden the top of this beautiful mountain.

AMA DABLAM - 6812m, October 26, 2018

Hello everyone, Angelo these days is involved in the takeover attempt of Ama Dablam. Finally, after long and grueling wait, two days ago could start climbing. First night in Camp 1, second night in Camp 2 where there is also at this time. Tomorrow, weather and conditions permitting, they will attempt the summit. The boys need our moral support, and strength and courage, we can not wait to return to know you are happy.

AMA DABLAM - THE MATTERHORN OF THE HIMALAYAS, October 20, 2018

As you know in recent days Angelo had to give up the initial project that led him to Nepal with the aim to climb Pumori, for the reasons explained to us. However, he has not lost his nerve and decided to take part in another expedition that will see him in the coming weeks in climbing of what is considered "one of the most beautiful mountains in the world", Ama Dablam whose meaning in Nepali language is "Mother with necklace", also known as the "Matterhorn of the Himalayas. It's prestigious mountain whose ascent is coveted for both its beauty and for the technical difficulty, less exposed to the risks of the objective Pumori, such as avalanches, of stones discharges, crevices, etc. Known as the Himalayan Matterhorn. Ama Dablam, the sacred mountain, stands majestically in Kumbu valley with its 6814 meters standing out with its unmistakable silhouette beyond the village of Pangboche. Wonderful! In these days of waiting for the new permit Angelo is located at the base camp and try to keep fit and acclimated with forays to the camp 1.

Main team arrived at BC on 11 Nov (4 others arrived earlier).

Matthew Stanbridge was an independent climber that came earlier.

Report from Jagged Globe website:

Conditions on the mountain were good. The Dablam serac looked benign, with a corridor on it’s righthand side up the headwall. The rock ridge to Camp 2 was virtually free of snow and ice, offering the enticing prospect of sun-kissed, solid granite scrambling. Was it really going to be Fire and Ice? Approach shoes for the rock and a switch at Camp 2, to boots with altitude and crampons for snow? Pemchhiri had recent experience, just days earlier, with Robert, extending his Cholatse expedition for a Khumbu double. Young Sherpas, Gyan and Babu, keen to add Ama Dablam to their logbooks, confirmed our hopes. The season so far had been dry but high winds had also been a feature. With our summit phase scheduled for December, we knew we would have the mountain to ourselves, yet we required a calm weather window to keep the windchill of low temperatures at bay.

Patience was needed, as was further acclimatisation, a four day rotation phase. We headed up for a night at advanced base, continuing the next day, past Camp 1 at 5700m, to gain 5800m along the fixed ropes, before returning for a first night there. Camp 2 and the legendary Yellow Tower were the next days objectives. With just light rucksacks for the day and light footwear, we set out with high hopes and warming rock. Camp 2 lies just beyond the crux rock pitch on the tower and even fixed ropes make this a gasping experience at 5900m. Andrew tackled it cleanly, to the amusement of the Sherpas, as he found a precarious resting place, without weighting the safeguarding, fixed line. As a single pitch at sea level it would weigh in as a quality HVS. I weaved my way, following his example, chuffing like a steam train, to latch the finishing edge and a moment of ridiculous glory.

We slid down the solid lines and tailed the traverse ropes to regain Camp 1 and I insisted we spend another night there. At least the next day back to base would see us served one of Kanchha’s feasts.

Rest and active recovery were the order of the next couple of days, even a slow walk to 5200m was imposed. My mantra of “listen to the mountain” and “listen to yourself” was repeated, to focus peoples’ minds in readiness for the challenge to come. We discussed clothing and tactics to prevent the very real risk of ‘cold injury’ and Mark and I made daily checks on the mountain forecast via WiFi and 4G at signal point – not quite from the comfort of the mess tent but not too far from base. We tracked an upcoming weather window.

It takes maturity to listen carefully enough to one’s feelings about a summit bid. Ewan and Mark chose with the wisdom of their years of mountain experience. We will share other summit bids in the future. With Kamchanu Sherpa paired with Tony, Rakesh with Hannah, Rinji with Andrew, Pem to lead with Fatima, allowing me mobility, we left base, aiming to slowly gain Camp 2 in two days.

The night at Camp 2 was going to be a short one, with a start scheduled for 2am. Stars lit the inky night and the winds were light, so the stoves were lit at 1am. Crampons scratched on the rock of the Grey Tower but soon runnels of snow and ice got our hearts thumping, blood pumping and lungs panting. Hand warmers slipped into gloves kept fingers nimble enough to manage ascender switches. Surely sunrise would bring some warmth? Tony knew himself well enough from Seven Summits and more to retreat to base in control. The remainder plugged on, yearning for daylight.

The Mushroom Ridge had little snow but dawn brought the steep barrier of ice cliffs below the level playing field, where prior to an avalanche in 2006 a camp used to be situated. We strained up the lines and there before us lay 500m of headwall.

Set at forty to fifty degrees the slope to summit made it seem close, it wasn’t. In tune with Pem, it took little more than a nod upwards and a few words to confirm our window of opportunity was open wide enough to proceed, even though we knew, after six hours of effort, we needed four more for the ascent, with a further five to slide the lines back down, minimum.

The Sun joined us for our slow-motion promenade. I drifted upwards, passing pairings, looking carefully. Near the Dablam, Fatima and Pem turned around, only four hundred metres left and she’s strong but smart too. Andrew continued, two thousand metres higher than his European best. Rinji led and I kept an imaginary elastic, ten double steps long, between Andrew and me. The summit ridge line edging closer. Hannah persisting behind us with every step an effort of will.

The summit vista was huge. The task of descending was no small matter either, requiring care and concentration. There was soup and sleep waiting for us, the end of a big day. Only hours later we were down at base.

Jamie Holding, IFMGA

From Darchula they trekked to Huti, Sunsera, Tusarpani and Dumlin. They had planned to climb Api from the north via the "normal route" (NW Ridge), but once they reached Dumlin, local people told them it's impossible to go to the north side. They were told that the road to BC on north is damaged because of landslides. They could have gone by walk and cross the river with ropes, but it would have been too dangerous because of rockfall. They returned to Dumlin and tried to find another way.

Locals told them there was an alternative trail to BC over hills, but it would have taken too much time and they were unsure about the condition of bridges on this trail. They thought of flying to BC by helicopter, but helicopter was too expensive, since there are no helicopters in the far west of Nepal.

They eventually went back to Darchula and took jeeps to Pari Bagar (3-4 hours from Darchula). From Pari Bagar they started the 4-day trek to south BC of Api. First day to Ghunsa, second day to Khayekot, third day to Dhaulo Odar, fourth day to Thusa (3800m/BC). Base camp was at a beautiful location, they were surrounded by 6000m mountains. They went to west side of Api, slept at 4300m on grass side (C1), then went up to 5100m among crevasses (C2) on a glacier. There was a bad thunderstorm that night, with a lot of flashes. 

From C2 they went north without tents for a pass. They wanted to find a way to the north side for a possible climbing route. Vit Auermuller reached the pass at 5500m, but saw that conditions on north side were unfavourable. The slope on the north was very steep, the weather looked unstable and they didn't have enough time to continue expedition on the north. Climbing the South Face of Api was out of question, too difficult, too steep. 

They abandoned the expedition and went back to Pari Bagar in 2 days via Khayekot. From Pari Bagar they took a jeep to Seri. From there to Hanidori by bus where they spent a night, then bus to Chitwan. Spent 2 days in Chitwan before they returned to Kathmandu.

Kelley and Billet left high camp (ABC) at 2:30 am on 22 Feb. They bived at 5200m approx on 23 Feb. Another biv at 5600m approx on 24 Feb. They spent night in their tent at around 5900m on 25, 26 and 27 Feb due to bad weather. They left for summit around 2 pm and got to the top at 5 pm on 28 Feb. They stayed on the summit for about 15 minutes (it was very cold and windy), then descended to 5900m camp where they reached around 8 pm. They spent the night there. Next day, 1 March they went down to their high camp where they arrived around midnight. The following day, 2 March descended to BC. There was not any sign of the BC, everything was swept away by an avalanche while they were climbing. They climbed in alpine-style using ropes most of the line but no fixed ropes.

Route grade was 4, most of the route - ice mixed climb. Summit day weather was windy. Most of the climbing days were cloudy, windy and deep snow.

It is the third attempt of Kelley on Chhopa Bamare. He was on the mountain in 2017 Dec, high point east summit 5900m approx on 24 Dec. Difficult route too hard for him to climb alone. 

In 2018 March, high point at 4600m on 14 March -  heavy snow; left 18 March. 


FB report by Ben Gustave about the First Ascent of Chhopa Bamare

Chhopa Bamare (6109m) - First Ascent - winter expedition : 09/02/19-03/03/19

The unusual snow conditions of this year completely changed the expedition. It’s been said that this was the snowiest winter in 25 years.

From Katmandu, it took us 7 days to reach base camp instead of 3. The bus got stuck on the way to the last village Lamabagar, 2 of our 3 porters let us down as soon as they saw a few cm of snow on the trail, we then had to move back and forward to carry all the food and gear for a 30 days to expedition. From BC we only took the minimum of gear to high camp, but it still took us 3 more days.

We started the climb on the 22nd of February at 2 am. The weather forecast was good with a few days of clear weather without snowfall, we would soon find out that it was completely wrong. Starting by climbing the spur following some snow gully and a bit of mixed climbing before a glacier traverse, we reached the bottom of the south face at 6:30 am. We climbed till 17:30 when the snowfall was too intense to continue. There was no place to bivy and small avalanches started to slide down the face. We cut a very small ledge (the biggest we could but just enough to stand on it), attached ourselves to an anchor and put the tent as a bivy bag over our head. Very long and exhausting night standing up in the cold with spindrift rolling over our head.

Quite exhausted the next morning, we only did a few pitches before the snow began to fall again and we bivied once again on the face, but on a larger ledge this time.

The 3rd day led us to the top of south face and could finally see the summit of Choppa Bamare. We went down the ridge and put the tent around 100-150m below the summit thinking that we could make an attempt the following day.

Then weather turned bad with heavy and strong winds we stayed stuck on the ridge for 3 nights at 6000m unable to leave the tent.

The last morning, almost out of gaz and food, we were reading to go down but The sun started to shine so we rushed up to the summit. We reached it after a few hours of climbing on the 28th of February (last day of Himalayan winter).

The following day was a long day of rappelling down the south face and the spur to reach our high camp. We had to dig a 1.5m of snow to find the back pack that we left behind with some gaz and food.

On our way down to base camp we noticed that the entire valley had been washed off by avalanches. There was no trace of our base camp, we lost everything that was there (tent, climbing gear, sleeping bags, pads, etc...). If we would have been there we would probably be under a few meter of snow at this moment. The next day we ran down to the closest village of yakers. They welcomed us like only Nepali can do and fed us with huge plates of dal baht.

The route is named "Seto Hi’um" which means white snow in nepali as a reminder of these incredible snowfall. We graded it TD (1150m, VI, M4, WI4). It’s a really good climb with a lot of pitches of WI3 / 3+ and a few harder ones. There is still lot of amazing lines that could be open on this south face as long as the conditions are in.

A big thanks to John, my climbing partner, for who it was the 3rd attempt on the mountain for giving me the opportunity to join him and realize the dream of many climbers: a first ascent in the Himalayas. We never met before and this month of expedition went really smoothly especially with the harsh conditions we had. Thanks to Devendra as well, the only porter that stayed we with us up to base camp and helped us so much for everything.
ACCIDENTS: Billet got frostbite on both toes. The right toe blistered and burst out taking off the nail. He said it will be fine. He was first admitted to Swacon International and moved to Norvic International Hospital on 7 March.

Climb abandoned on 28/09 due to too much snow on the route for the Sherpas to fix and bad weather forecast. High winds were predicted until 08/10 at least.

First time team ascended to C1, it took them 12 hours as due to the heavily crevassed glacier route finding was difficult. They fixed the rope from the bottom of the Eiger to the glacier. For glacier crossing they roped up to get to C1. As they arrived at C1 just before sunset, they set up camp without noticing that they had placed it in between many crevasses - that's why they moved it when they went up for their second rotation.

Bierling arrived at BC on 13/09. She trekked in with SST group via Damphus and French Pass.

On 18/09, the group ascended again to C1, where they stayed the night. On 19/09 they were planning to reach C2 and stay the night there; however, when they reached around 6,000m they found out that Dawa Gyalzen Sherpa of SST had been killed in an avalanche, which the rope fixing Sherpas triggered at around 6700m (on the rib just above C2). They descended to C1, where they spent another night. They tagged C2 at 6550m on 20/09. Back to BC on 20/09.

Team left BC for a possible summit push on 26/09. It took them 11 hours to reach C1 as the route had been covered in snow. On 27/09, the team ascended to C2 where they stayed the night. The SST was also at C2 and on 28/09 four of their Sherpas and the three Kobler & Partner Sherpas started fixing the rope. However, due to the deep snow, they only reached 6700m and abandoned their attempt. The entire team descended to C1, where they stayed the night and were back at BC on 29/09.

Specht reached lower C1 on 06/09 during first acclimatisation rotation. He had an old shoulder injury and decided that he could not continue. He left BC on 12/09.

Team left BC on 02/10 (trekked to Marpha in 11 hours).

Smt 18/19  by all members at 1:30 pm.
 
Intended SE Ridge first, which was attempted in the past years by a French team (Patrick Wagnon/Dorian Labaeye) and last year by a "Grit and Rock" sponsored team. Found the access to the ridge in too dry and dangerous conditions this year, so changed their plans for the SW Ridge instead.

Ascent schedule: 
October 15: BC to C1.
October 16: C1 to C2.
October 17: C2 to C3. Main difficulties begun on that day.
October 18: main difficulties ended on that day, then team exited by Bruce Normand
  route. The two ropes reached the top in the middle of a stronger wind than 
  desired: it blew with a force of between 40 and 50 km/h, with gusts of up to 70
  km/h. Bivouacked back to 6400m on that evening.
October 19: abseiling and back crossing a lot from 6400m bivy to 5000 meters C1.
October 20: back to BC.

Difficulties maintained from 6100 to 6600m, which is the second part of the route. Stretches there of AI5, V grade rock and also mixed terrain. The final part of the climb coincides with Bruce Normand's route 2005.

Name of the route: “Bizipenak”, which means "experiences" in Basque language.

Mikel Zabalza, who added with Drangnag Ri his 37th expedition outside Europe, says that Bizipenak "is one of the most beautiful routes that I have climbed in the Himalayas."

Team left BC on October 25.

Other peaks: a peak of 5960m with no name on the border with Tibet, east of Langdung Peak, for acclimatization.
ACHIEVMENT: 1st ascent of the SW Ridge in alpine style

ROUTEMEMO:
Sep 28  Aarival in KTM
Oct 01  From KTM to Beni by jeep
Oct 02  From Beni to Phole by Jeep
Oct 06  Reached BC at 3800m from Gurja Khani (3061m)

Oct 10  In the evening, avalanche occurred, killing all members and hired 
        sleeping at BC.

Oct 11  Aaency was expecting one trekking member (Jeong Joon-M) to come back down to the village of Gurja Khani at noon but he didn't. Agency tried to contact BC without success, so sent one of their guide (looking after an other sick trekking member at Gurja Khani) who went until a very few kms down to the BC. Saw already 1 body and that something happened...

Oct 12  Agency sent 16 villagers with staff plus sent an helicopter. All reached BC and discovered the whole tragedy. 9 dead bodies, clothes and tents remnants were scathered 200m to 500m below the BC, bodies still in their sleeping bags. Rescue team noticed the bodies were wet, even the money inside the clothes was wet. There was no remnants of any kind of snow avalanche around the BC, but the grass was completely flattened downwards. Agency planned in that evening during 6h how to rapatriate the bodies. Helicopters were sent in that evening to Pokhara.

Oct 13  Evacuation big mission was completed in a short time due to delicate weather conditions. Rescue team and villagers also helping at BC site.

Oct  16  Bodies were sent back to South Korea.

Cause of the disaster : 

At first many hypothesis were spread into the world press: landslide, avalanche, storm, lake flood, severe cyclonic storm "Titli"...

https://www.facebook.com/karna.lama.54/posts/10156635349099705

The villagers below hadn't heard nor seen anything during the October 10 to 11 night: not any sound, not any river sudden flood (=> lake flood). Saw trees breaking on their way up when reaching nearby BC. Most likely cause of the disaster seems to be an avalanche blast following the fall of a serac. Spanish source of Desnivel and Korean sources (agency told) both agree it must be a serac blast phenomenon.
 
https://www.desnivel.com/expediciones/que-les-sucedio-realmente-a-los-9-muertos-del-gurja/

Sadly helicopters couldn't fly over the area above the BC. One of the helicopters camera was shooting images downwards from the front of the heli to assist the rescue; it was cloudy weather on the 13th of October. There was no helicopter flight done afterwards to seek for the origin of the disaster above BC, since it was already very expensive to organize the rescue itself. So there were no image gathered from the zone from where might have originated the phenomenon.

Accident even more surprising since in 2017, Kim Chang-Ho had made a recce of the area to decide for the safest place to establish BC. BC was established north-northeastwards in the valley straight above Gurja Khani, the large water basin catchment lying immediately southwestwards of Gurja Himal large South Face (there are two large water basin catchments collecting the waters of the south face of Gurja Himal, an eastern and a western one). Access from Gurja Khani consists in walking up via that valley talweg towards the very footstep of the SE Face (c.3345m on Googlearth), then go up a 5 hundred meters NNE via grass slopes to the BC site located on a small ledge.

The upper part of that valley is a steep wall forming the left part of Gurja South Face. From 3345m to 4500m are grassy slopes, then from it to the summit is a steep 2600m high wall. Northeastwards to the BC is a couloir showing a narrow steep section from helicopter, and above which lies the 4500m high footstep of the proper SE Face where seracs seem to have landed. Heli survey of that upper zone may have allowed to identify the exact location of the serac(s) fall and likely found the remnants at the aforementionned 4500m high place.
ACCIDENTS: Death of 4 members, 4 hired and 1 trekking member on 10-11 Oct in the night

Brian Jackson (by email) - 30 Nov 2018

Hello Billi,

Please see the basic ascent report below for the Himalayan Database. If you need any more information, please let me know...

12 hrs on a bus from Kathmandu to Lamabagar in the North-South Rolwaling Valley, one of the 7 hidden valleys of Nepal and home of the Yeti.

From Lamabagar, we trekked with 9 porters, 2 climbing Sherpas (Mingma Dorje Sherpa and Mindu Sherpa), 3 cook crew and 1 cook (Amrit Rai) from Lukla region + 5 local porters for 4 days to the last village of Lapche where the valley splits. At this point the 5 local porters left us to return to Lamabagar.

We then headed to the Tibetan border on the northeast valley from Lapche. We trekked up the very steep valley side to the hidden plateau between the two valleys to our base camp at 4836m (N 28° 8.6478' E 86° 11.4909'), reaching it on November 20th.

Rest, acclimatisation, jumar practice and puja ceremony at base camp.

Moved on to ABC at 5300m in the cul-de-sac of the Nepal/Tibet border of mountains (N 28° 9.9647' E 86° 11.0147') reaching it on November 23rd.

Korlang Pari Tippa lay before us with an East and West Summit on the 500m long ridge. We looked at the East summit as it was the highest, but could not see a route up the twin towers of broken shattered blocks as it is practically unclimbable. We therefore chose to climb the lower West Summit which still had falling rock and difficult shattered rock traverses.

We reached the Western summit of Korlang Pari Tippa North (5574m - N 28° 10.3081' E 86° 10.9266') on Sunday 25th November at 9:20 am using 350m of fixed line. Those who summitted were: Brian Jackson, Gwyn Griffiths, Daniel Walker, Catherine Husted, Mingma Dorje Sherpa and Mindu Sherpa.

Kind regards,
Brian Jackson

BC  15/10  4200m 
HC  20/10  5350m 
Smt 21/10  by Jankowski, Finden, Jones, Townsden, De Lathouder and 5 Sherpas.

Yubra 6035m (unclimbed according to NMA list) on Nepa Maps as Langtang Yubra 6048m. 
Group had some acclimatization walks near Kyanjin Gompa.

BC was located low at 4200m behind moraine of Lirung Glacier. Beyond BC access was very difficult. High camp (HC) located on the top edge of the Kimshung Icefall at 5350m. From BC to HC not through the icefall, but via a big ramp of scree and rocks west of icefall "Horrible way". Some risk of rockfall, lots of boulders. Some short pitches of rope fixed at the ramp. Beyond HC a glacial basin "like the Western Cwm" on Everest.

Team left HC for the summit at 3:30 am. Crampon point 30 minutes from HC, then long traverse on the left hand side of basin. Middle of basin heavily crevassed, but they walked around crevasse area.
 
Headwall starts at 5700m. Max. 80° headwall of snow and ice. They used ice screws and snow bars for belaying.

Team summited between 10:45 am-12:15 pm. Townsden was first of team members to summit at 11:30 am, last to summit was Lathouder at 12:15 pm. They had outstanding weather on the whole trip, also on summit day blue skies. Sherpas had fixed most of the route two days before. Fix ropes on entire headwall, about 400m.

The three radios of the group all failed at same time and Ledet at HC was worried about group. No contact for some hours. All back to HC safely at 5:20 pm.

Kosztelny had a knee injury previously which was why he couldn't attempt the summit. He left from Kyanjin to KTM on 15/10 by helicopter.

Megan Ward got sick at the bottom of headwall (probably AMS). Ledet took her back to HC, where she was given oxygen for the night.

Hawkins, Andersson and Riley decided to turn back with Ledet and Ward. They were spooked by the steep headwall.

Everyone descended to BC on 22/10. Rest day on 23/10 and back to Kyanjin Gompa on 24/10. Left BC on 24/10 for Kyanjin Gompa, then Langshisa Ri.

Oxygen: Taken and used for medical reasons by Megan Ward at HC.

Fixed rope: short sections between BC and HC (not more than 80m in total), 400m at headwall.

ROUTEMEMO:
BC  25/10  5100m on Ripimo Glacier 
ABC 26/10  5400m
Smt 28/10  by all 3 members at 4 pm.

Ascent schedule:

The trio first intended the Drangnag Ri South Face, which is still unattempted, but abandoned the idea because "we found the approach very complicated and we did not have enough time." Meanwhile Zabalza intended at first the SE Ridge before shifting attention onto the SW Ridge being less dry.

Oct 25: From Na Village to BC

Oct 26: From BC to ABC (moved with same tent) 

Oct 27: "loaded the material to the wall and climbed several lengths, among them the most difficult of the track: a 6c + collapsed chimney that Ruiz Blin solved as if it were next to the ground. That night we slept again in the ABC, where the temperature dropped to -16ºC."

Oct 28: "left the ABC at 3:00 in the morning so, at dawn, we would be climbing longs from the top with difficulties between V + and 6b. Towards 11 o'clock we reached the beginning of the SE Ridge (about 6000m), where sections of ice and rock alternated, with conditions that varied greatly depending on the orientation. After several hours of climbing, the edge seemed never to end. Sections of IV and V grade with others of very bad rock made the climbing really delicate. In addition, not being very well acclimatized, the awareness of the height was accentuated at every step. We reached the summit, exhausted, at around 4:00 pm. Our idea was to detour the ridge and go down where we had climbed but, due to how long it was, we decided to change our plan to descend rappelling towards the glacier by a channel on the west side starting 30m near the summit.

The descent turned out to be very long, and what seemed like a quick retreat became endless rappels. The lower we got, the poorer the rock was. Thus, I do not think it is the best option for descent. Around 23:00, after about 18 rappels, we landed on the glacial crevasse where we continued sailing in search of the heat of the store. 
On the glacier, we still had to mount several rappels to finally get back to the store at 12 o'clock at night, after 21 hours of incredible but strenuous activity."

Route: “Bihâna” (amanacer/breaking dawn), 1500m, ED+, 6c+. First 500m on a granite wall, then 1000m on the ridge. Descent by a couloir on the west side.

No proof for summit: "Sorry. We went down almost at night and did not take pictures."

Team left BC on Oct 29.

Records: 1st ascent of the SE Face-SE Ridge.

Fixed rope: none

BC  08/09  5300m
C1  15/09  5700m
C2  16/09  6300m
C3  25/09  7200m
Smt 29/09  by Urken Sherpa at 6 pm
Smt 30/09  by Nelson, Morrison, Kalisz, Simpson, Phu Tashi Sherpa, 
           Ila Nuru Sherpa at 1:45 pm.

Rope-fixing Sherpas (Urken, Palden, Phura Sherpa) went from C2 towards the summit in one day. They fixed from 7300m to a little bit below the summit. They ran out of rope and Palden and Phura turned around about 100m below the top. Urken Sherpa was keen to summit and continued on his own. He descended to C3, where he arrived at 8 pm. Palden and Phura were back at C3 together with the members.

30/09 summit team left C3 at 2 am. Nelson and Morrison headed out without supplemental O2 as Nelson thought it was a good idea to summit without oxygen. Both of them were carrying their skis and they were in ski-boots. When they reached 8200m (base of couloir) the snow changed. Crust was less firm and they broke in about knee-deep. The Sherpas had deposited some oxygen at the base of the couloir and Morrison and Nelson decided to put on oxygen. They spent about 45 minutes on the summit and Nelson and Morrison started their ski descent.

They put their skis on on the summit and skied down to C2. Conditions in the couloir were varied. Breakable crust, ice. Couloir wide enough to ski. No rappel. Skiing the Lhotse Face was fine. They reached C2 at 6 pm.

On 01/10 Morrison and Nelson continued to ski down to C1. They started out without skins, but as they were pulling heavy duffle bags they put on their skins to be able to drag the duffle bags down. They dropped their loads at 5900m and then they skinned and skied down to the top of the icefall at 5600m.

Kalisz and Simpson also descended to C2 after the summit. Kalisz reached C2 at 8 pm, Simpson at 7:30 pm.

Team left BC on 04/10 by helicopter.

Fixed Rope: 
  Two icefall doctors (Dawa and Nima) fixed the icefall in 2 ½ days. 
  They only used one ladder. No rope fixed between C1 and C2. 
  Sherpas fixed from bergschrund (6600m) all the way to the summit.
  Total ca. 3000m (with icefall).

Oxygen:
  Taken and used from 8200m to top to C3 by Nelson
  From 8200m to top to C2 by Morrison
  From C3 to top to C2 by Kalisz and Simpson
  From C2 to top to C2 by Sherpas.

Climbing route: S Face to saddle, then W Ridge to summit.

25/10 BC at 4300m at Langshisa Kharka. 27/10 From BC to high camp (5600m). Rest day on 28/10 at high camp.

HC located right on top of a ramp. Ramp maybe 45° with lots of rockfall. On 29/10 they left for the summit from HC at 4 am. Jankowski, Finden, Townsden, Ledet, Andersson got to high point 6200m just below the saddle at 12:30 pm. Lakpa Nuru Sherpa was the only one to summit at around 11:30 am and waited for them. But members still had at least 1.5 hours to go to summit. They were tired. Distance between HC and summit was longer than anticipated and it was getting late in the day. They decided to turn back. Last members back to HC at 6:15 pm.

Ward and Riley stayed at BC because of sickness.

Jones got spooked by rockfall, didn't want to go any further. Lathouder moved slowly and Ledet asked her to return. Lathouder and Jones descended with Mingma Sherpa.

Team rturned to BC on 30/10, rested on 31/10 and left BC on 01/11 to Kyanjin.

Biv1 23/10  6400m shortly underneath ridge

Smt  25/10  by David Lama at 10 am.

Lama climbed from ABC to Bivouac 1 during the night. Lower part of route prone to ice and rock fall. On 23/10 he stayed in the tent the whole day, it had 80km/h wind. He had started from ABC at 1 am and reached Biv 1 at 6 am. Good climbing on mixed ground. Not much ice in lower wall, wall between 60-80°.

On 24/10 he started from Biv 1 at 6am. Reached Bivouac 2 some time in the afternoon. He gained the West Ridge at 6500m.

From Biv 2 he started at 6 am and reached the summit at 10 am.

Before the summit he left the West Ridge and climbed the North Face (90°, crux). Stayed on the summit only 5 minutes. On descent he rappelled down the same route, all the way to ABC. Arrived at ABC at 11 pm.

He carried 3 ropes, self belayed for 50m on 24/10 and for 100m on 25/10. All in all no perfect conditions, very cold on mountain (-25 to -30°C).

Hanslmayr and Haverstock reached ABC on 18/10. They never planned to go any higher on mountain.

On 30/10 Lama left BC for Namche Bazaar and on to climb Cholatse.

Climbing route: 
  West Face to West Ridge, last section on North Face just before summit.

Oxygen: Not taken, not used

He should have been with them, but he was too slow. He was supposed to use oxygen ,but didn't. They eventually borrowed another bottle from another Sherpa and shared the bottle between them. From Himalayan Times, 1 Oct 2018:

Chang Dawa Sherpa, Expeditions director at Seven Summit Treks shared that at least 69 climbers including 38 foreign members from his company climbed Mt Manaslu (8,163 m) this season. Among the summiteers, a team of Chinese climbers led by Wang Ping (Sula) successfully scaled Mt Manaslu on Saturday. “Cai Yanjuan, Cui Zhouping, Gexi Yangchu, He Honghu, Lou Rijia, Qui Zu, Rui Junjian, Zhou Yan, Ze Yong and Zhang Yang along with Mingma Thinduk Sherpa, Tendi Sherpa, Phurba Dorchi Sherpa, Tenzen Sherpa, Phurtenze Sherpa and Tashi Sherpa reached the summit point,” he said.

Summit team left C4 between 11 pm and 2 am. Conditions were little gusty and clear. Snow hard and crispy. They spent an average of around 20 minutes on the summit. Dawa Steven, Jung Thapa, Tang Qun, Xin-Yong Zhou, Xie Wei and Kenji Kasahara descended to BC where they arrived between 7 pm and 9 pm. The rest of the team descended to C2.

On the descent, Shu-Jie Gong took a wrong step and broke the outside bone of her ankle at about 7500m at about 11 am. Five Sherpas carried her down to C3, where they arrived 10 pm. On 30/09, all 13 Sherpas worked together and helped her down to BC, where they arrived at 2 am. It took them 19 hours. On 01/10 Gong took a helicopter from BC to Samagaon, where she stayed the night and on 02/10 helicopter to Kathmandu and got admitted to CIWEC.

On the way up, Dawa Steven Sherpa and Zhang were caught in an avalanche below C3. They were dragged with it for about 10m. They were clipped into the rope and got out of the avalanche.

Zhou and Xie skied down from the plateau (below the false summit) to about half way to C4 and walked down to C2.

Jian-Pei Shen reached BC. He left BC after five days as he was not up for the climb.

Song never intended to summit. He was the official expedition leader to stay at BC.

Team left BC on 01/10.

Tim Bogdanov reached summit at 10:30 am. He was trying speed record, but didn't succeed. He started from C4, summitted and went down to C1.

Party on 28 Sep reached summit between 5:30 and 7 am. There was a lot of traffic at the summit area and they reached summit at different times. They left C4 at 11 pm, summited and went to C3 which they reached at 6:30 pm.

Party on 29 Sep reached summit at around 6:30 am. Started from C4 at 11 pm, and went back to C3.

Daniel Toader reached 7400m on 28 Sep, but couldn't continue due to problems with his liver. He and Oxana flew from Samagaon to KTM on 2 Oct.

Nicolai Timofeev couldn't deal with technical difficulty of the icefall at 6000m and had to turn around on 18-Sep. He wasn't experienced enough.

Lakpa Tsering and Irena Alaeva wanted to paraglide from the summit, but the test flight failed and they abandoned the plan. Lakpa Tsering on the test flight on 16 Sep crashed into a crevasse, but was able to rescue himself out of the crevasse. He wanted to fly from BC to Samagaon. He had some pain, but nothing serious and he went on to summit later. According to Oxana, Lakpa Tsering climbed as a member.

Irena Alaeva claims to be the first person from the autonomous republic of Abkhazia to summit Manaslu.

According to Albu, ascent wasn't difficult, but the descent was. Albu ran out of water on the descent, he started to eat snow and was finally given some juice by a Sherpa. He went down all the way to BC, which took him 14 hours. Sherpas had taken all tents from high camps, so Radu had to descend the whole way. He reached BC at 7 pm.

Bethell some frostbite at toes, went to hospital in Kathmandu, but not serious. Albu spent one night in hospital in Kathmandu, some frostbite at face, forehead and big toe, not serious.

Matter came out trekking, Albu took heli from Samagaon on 30 Sep. Bethell by heli from Samagaon to Kathmandu on 01/10.

Albu had planned to climb Ama Dablam in a speed record alone after Manaslu, but it didn't work out because of exhaustion; he lost 10 kg on Manaslu and never tried Ama Dablam.

From the Himalayan Times
Czech Climber Missing on Manaslu, 30 Sept 2018
by Rajan Pokhrel

KATHMANDU: A foreign climber has been missing on Mt Manaslu since last evening with bad weather hampering search and rescue efforts throughout the day today, the expedition organiser said.

According to Tej Bahadur Thapa, Managing Director at Mount Treks Nepal Pvt. Ltd, Roman Hlavko (43) from the Czech Republic went missing while descending from the summit point of the 8163-metre peak.

The mountaineer reportedly made it to the summit point along with three fellow mountaineers in the afternoon, he said, adding that other climbers only realised that Roman was missing when they arrived at Camp III.

“Roman, a part of the 14-member expedition, was last seen near Camp IV while descending from the top of the 8163-metre mountain,” Thapa quoted other climbers as saying.

Once the climber was reported missing this morning, a four-member rescue team from Seven Summit Treks has been deployed on foot, the official said. The team, however, failed to make it to above Camp II as the route was hit by avalanches.

Summit team, Kay and Mizelli, Kunga Sherpa and Ang Kaji Sherpa left C2 at 2:15 am. Kay and Kunga Sherpa reached 6800m; however, Kay was not using his skis and found it too hard as he was breaking in. They turned back and descended to BC.

On 06/10 he was picked up by helicopter and flown to Kathmandu. He has frostbite on both hands, and will probably lose parts of two fingers.

Garber and Noichl-Platzer continued to the summit in good weather. They spent about 10 minutes on the summit (it was very windy) and then descended on skis to C1 and then walked to BC.

Feichtinger and Boeck reached 6180m. They had stayed at a lower C2 at 6,080m as they were both feeling sick with an upset stomach. On 05/10, they ascended for another 100m to take photos and then skied down to C1 and continued to BC walking.

Trittler and Locher stayed at C2 together on 29/09. They left C2 at about 5 am to go for the summit. After about 200m going towards the summit Locher lost the skins of his skis and decided to turn back. He skied down to C2 to wait for Trittler. Trittler carried on skinning up. He was completely on his own. He stayed for about 10 minutes on the summit. He skied down to C2, picked up Locher and they skied down to C1 together from where they walked to BC. They arrived at BC at around 5 pm.

Locher, Patrizia Russ and Kusang (with snow shoes) left BC again (skinning) for a second summit push on 01/10. On 02/10 Russ turned back at around 5800m as she was low on energy due to an upset stomach she had been suffering from.

On 03/10 Kusang and Locher left C2 at 3 am. The conditions were not as good as on 30/09 as the snow had been blown away. The ground was hard and icy. Locher used ski crampons and Kusang used snow shoes. They stayed for about 5 minutes on the summit as it was very windy. Trittler skied all the way to C1 on the way down they broke down all camps. They walked to BC, where they arrived at 4 pm.

Spieth reached 5800m on 29/09. She did not carry on due to a cold. She was feeling too weak to carry on.

Patrizia and Rebecca Russ reached 6100m (C2) on 29/09. This rotation was intended to be an acclimatization rotation and they did not join the spontaneous summit push of Trittler and Locher. Rebecca Russ did not join the second summit push as she was feeling too weak due to stomach problems.

Team left BC on 05/10.

TERMNOTE: Abandoned at 6400m due to altitude, frostbite and exhaustion
ROUTEMEMO:
Approach: Flight to Dhanghadi, 12 hour drive to Chainpur, 2 hour drive to Talkot, trekking from Talkot.

BC  05/10  3955m
ABC 10/10  4725m
C1  20/10  5400m in bergschrund
HPt 21/10  6400m by all members.

Climb abandoned on 21/10 due to altitude, frostbite and exhaustion.

Alpine-style ascent, no oxygen, no Sherpas. Planned ski descent from summit. 

SW Face steep face, a lot of snow (40cm), good climbing 45°-60°. Team climbed unroped. Highpoint 6400m on 21/10. They started from C1 at 5:30 am. Reached highpoint at approximately 10:30 am. Sayers had slight frostbite at toes (nerve damage, but no lasting damage expected). Everyone felt tired and struggled with altitude. Everyone back to BC at 6:30 pm. A lot of spindrift with small avalanches between highpoint and C1.

They hired porters from the region. "The west is quite new to tourism, no porter structure like at other areas. Porters carry a much lighter load."

Porter fatal accident happened on 30/10 between 3-4 pm. A porter called Puna (from Talkot) on the way out at 2200m fell down the trail for about 600 feet. It was a wide trail, but with a steep edge. He was alone at the time of accident. According to police statement he tripped on a rock and lost his footing. Rest of porter were upset especially his brother. He was most angry and the others were following. They blamed members for the death and threatened to kill them. They picked up rocks and threatened to stone them to death, but didn't throw. In total 15-20 porters. Members and porters recovered the body with ropes.

Team left BC on 28/10.

Oxygen: Not taken, not used.

Fixed rope: None
ACCIDENTS: 1 porter (Puna) fell of the trail on 30/10 at 2200m during return march

BC  26/10  4700m at Dhaulagiri I basecamp
Smt 28/10  by both members.

"We had nonstop action during 11 days from Darbang to Darbang, like snails, carrying evetything. Just one porter helped us till Italian camp”. Reached Dhaulagiri I basecamp in 5 days, reaching it on October 26.

On October 28 had a 18-hour climbing day, starting at midnight. "We climb up by the red line (70°/M5-6) and descended by ski the yellow line (55° in some parts)."
  
Most expeditions used the same easiest route so far on Sita Chuchura: E Ridge from south side. The 1st ascent team (SITA-703-01) and 2nd ascent team (SITA-923-01) likely used the same lower part of the route, in the upper part the latter going towards harder conditions choose to avoiding the face by the right (50/60° slopes).

Tosas route on the lower part likely lies further to the left (or more "southernly") to these successful teams, then in the final part joins either 1st or 2nd ascent team's same route. Down it likely uses the same route.

Team left BC on October 30.

Route: E Ridge from south, with a new variant/route in the lower part.

Oxygen: Not taken, not used

Records: possible first ascent of a new variant in the lower part of the route; first ski descent of the peak via the E Ridge from south.

Fixed rope: None

Camp Coordinates:
  C1:  N 27.757616, E 87.925472
  C2:  N 27.757663, E 87.922951
  C3:  N 27.757104, E 87.919571
  Smt: N 27.758384, E 87.919228, 6328m 
  C4:  N 27.765087, E 87.924086

Team set out from C3 at 9 am and summited at 10:30 am, then descended separate line (N Ridge) to Camp 4, reached at 5:30 pm.

We climbed the East Face and descended the North Ridge. We had considered descending the same line of descent, but had scouted the alternative snowy North Ridge. We realized it would be time consuming and dangerous to rappel and downclimb the line of ascent because of complicated terrain, multiple required rappels, and some observed rockfall.

ED2, AI5 M6. From Camp 1 at base of East Face, followed 2 gully systems through 4 cliffbands with ice and mixed climbing to pass each cliff band. Switched gullies to the north after 2 cliffbands via a snowfield. 10 pitched-out pitches, about 14 simulclimbed pitches of steep snow to 90 degrees. 8cm of snow the first night. Temperatures down to -25C at night at Camp 3, with steady 25 knot winds on summit ridge. Several solid ice pitches, some loose rock on mixed pitches. One rockfall event down lower line of ascent after passing. 5 full-length rappels and steep downclimbing to descend.

ROUTEMEMO:
Team summited on 25 Sept at 10:45 am, then descended to BC arriving at 6:45 pm.

The route to climb the summit of Tsartse is the south-west side of the mountain through the summit of Tashi Kang I (6386m). Starting from Camp 1 near the South Ridge of Tashi Kang they traversed on the snow face of the east side of the mountain, and reached the col between Tashi Kang North and Tashi Kang I. Then they walked on the plateau to the base of Tsartse, and climbed the SW Face reaching the summit of Tsartse.

Bowie left C4 at 11.30 am. He was climbing without oxygen and caught up with the rest of the group after about half an hour. He had to wait in line for about an hour and decided to go down. He met Tendi Chhumbi Sherpa (with Findik) who grabbed him and told him to turn around and continue to climb. Eventually he was able to pass the rest of the group and continued to follow the He Jing, who was also attempting to climb without supplemental oxygen. As Bowie was not wearing a downsuit and no 8000m boots, his main concern was to stay warm. It was completely calm. The group was moving very slowly. Mingma David, Gesman Tamang and Gyalge were fixing rope at the front. He spent about 20 minutes on the summit and descended to C4, where he arrived at 7 pm. Stayed for the night

Don acclimatised on the other side of the mountain and slept a couple of nights at 4800m on 04/04 and 05/04. He then went around to the north side and had his first night at C1 on and then stayed five nights at C2 he touched C3 on that rotation.

Malaysian rescue:

Bowie heard about Wui Kin Chin still being up on the mountain. They waited for the oxygen to be dropped by helicopter. However Gobal Rescue, the insurance company, refused to drop the oxygen for insurance reasons. As there was no oxygen, Bowie went to BC on 24/04. In the meantime, Chin’s wife organised a helicopter. On 25/04 Simrik arrived. Chin was at 7500m. The B3 took Nims, Mingma David, Gesman Tamang and Gyalgen to C3 by long-line. They then climbed up to C4 and reached Chin at about 7 pm. The rescue team then dragged Chin down to C3, where they arrived in the morning of 26/04. Simrik then long-lined him to BC. According Bowie, Chin could not move his legs or bend them and his legs were frozen to the hips. Bowie flew to hospital with Chin.

ROUTEMEMO:
We started the climb for the NE Face of Chobutse Peak on Tuesday 7th May 2019, from our Advanced Base Camp (if it can be called this way) on the right side moraine (geographically) of Ripimo Glacier, at an altitute of aproximately 5000m. We found a place to traverse the unstable slopes of the glacier and we started towards the base of the NE at around 1:30 am. After aproximately 2 hours we reached the base and started to simul solo on easy terrain up the first part of the face. We encountered the first difficulties quite low, on a rockband. We roped up there and climbed technical terrain (around M5 max) for 4 pitches. Then we simul-climbed a long snowfield. The morning sun started to melt the snow and the conditions got a bit dangerous because of falling snow and rocks. We found a suitable place for a bivouac and set our First Light tent at around 1 pm. We rested the all day waiting for the shade or the dark to set in. The next morning we wanted to start at 2 am, but the strong wind and spindrifts made it very difficult to cook and pack the tent. We started at 4:30 am on mixed terrain. We climbed 2 long pitches until we encountered hard, technical climbing again. There was no solid ice formed on the slabs so we had to climb mixed ground, in some places hard to protect (maybe around M6 or a bit more...but hard to tell...it was more delicate than physical). In between the technical rock bands we had easy ice. We then traversed left a few ice runnels and reached a snow slope with rotten snow. We climbed for 3 more pitches on the snow to reach a narrow ridge. At the end of the ridge (arround 5950m) we tried to find shelter or a bivouac place because the wind was getting quite strong and we were getting tired. We couldn't dig a platform because the ice was too thin and there was rock slabs underneath. Kyriakos climbed 40m higher and tried to
find a place for a bivouac under an overhang, but the place was very unstable. Above us the terrain looked easier than before, but with long icy slopes that looked very physical. The next possible bivouac was probably on the N Ridge, exposed to wind and 300m higher. We decided that it was quite far for us to reach on that day so we started rappelling on a couloir facing east. Not finding any bivouac place lower, we kept on rapelling until the base of the couloir, then traversed over a hanging glacier (exposed terrain) and continued down climbing and rapelling until 1 am in the night. We then reached the base of the NE Face again and set our tent outside of objective dangers. The next day we continued our way down the Ripimo Glacier and back to Na village (our base camp).

Chekigo attempt:

We wanted to try to acclimatize on Chekigo peak because it was very close to Na village and our base camp. We had some pictures with previus attempt lines and routes on the S Face, but we managed to spot a virgin line in the middle of the face. It looked hard technically, but it looked challenging to us. We started from a bivouac under a big rock that protected us from potential ice fall from the big serac on the right side of the face. We climbed technical terrain at the base of the face and during the day we end up in a melting snowfield. It was very hard progress in the melting snow. We climbed very light, no bivouac gear, no tent. We only had water and some energy gels and some energy bars. We decided to abseil and go down (at 5800m) when we noticed that the climb would take us longer than we predicted.

When the team reached high point, they were on their second rotation. They did not intend to sleep at C2, but only touch it, which they did. They descended to ABC on 28/04, and on 29/04 they went to Tingri to rest, apart from Trump and the Weingartls, who stayed at ABC.

On 29/04, the Sherpas were intending to continue fixing the rope above C2. Mark Trump was going up with them to set up his C2. About 20m below C2, Phujung Bhote slipped and fell down for about 20 metres and then slipped into a crevasse. The other four Sherpas fixed some rope to abseil down to Phujung, but only found him dead.

On 29/04, expedition leader Szwed who was in Tingri with his team at the time, found out via the liaison officer that Phujung was killed in an accident. On 30/04 Szwed informed his team. On 30/04 liaison officer went back to Cho Oyu ABC to deal with the death and Szwed stayed in Tingri to coordinate the follow-up actions. After he received weather forecast from Innsbruck, which forecast unfavorable weather for summit push until 16 May, he decided to abandon climb. On 02/05 Szwed returned to ABC and on 03/05 the rest of the team joined him there. The Sherpas went up to C1 and C2 to bring the stuff down on 02/05 and team left BC on 05/05.

On 30/04, the two climbing Sherpas (Ang Dawa and Geli Sherpa) went back up to retrieve the body, but they did not have the equipment or strength to haul him out of the crevasse from 50 metres. They fixated Phujung’s body to the wall of the crevasse to leave him there in case somebody will come and get his body.

Climb abandoned on 02/05 due to bad weather and fatal accident

ROUTEMEMO:
BC  17/04  4000m (Japanese BC)
C1  09/05  5000m (on top of the ridge)

HPt 19/05  5600m by Colibasanu, Hamor and Gane.

Team stayed for two days at BC after arrival. Then they went to Dhaulagiri normal BC for one night and French Pass for one night and returned to BC. They started to fix the route from BC to C1 on the North Face. It took them six (non-consecutive) days to fix the route from 4,600m to C1 on the top of the ridge. The approach from BC to the bottom of the face was over moraine, a snowfield. The conditions on the face were very difficult – mixed rock and ice, but with very brittle ice so Colibasanu cleared the rock from the ice with a shovel to make it easier. They fixed about 600m of rope up to the ridge.

Once they had established their C1 on the top of the ridge on 09/05, they fixed to about 100m below C2 and returned to C1 to stay the night on 10/05. On 11/05, they finished fixing to C2 and put up the tent, but did not stay the night as it was too windy. The route on the ridge was not too difficult with rock slabs, ice and snow ridge. They descended to BC. They remained in BC for one week and waited for the wind to abate. 

On 17/05, they started their summit push even though the weather forecast was still predicting wind. When they woke up at C2 at 20/05 it first seemed calm, however, the tent was protected and they did not feel the wind. Higher up they saw the spindrifts and decided to abandon the expedition. They packed up their stuff and descended to BC the same day.

According to Horia, the problem of this route was the technical challenge with partially overhanging rock and ice. It was slow going and he thinks that it would probably help to be acclimatised before getting to the ridge. They often covered a long distance, but only gained 30 or 40 metres in altitude, which made the climb extremely cumbersome.

Climb abandoned on 20/05 due to being too windy and left BC on 21/05.

Fixed rope: 1000m

Oxygen: Not taken, not used


Facebook Post: Horia Colibasanu, 23 May 2019 (GOOGLE translation from Romanian)

I go down carefully, with the corners bobbing on the crumbly shales, plunging me from time to time into the frozen frost. Although here would go without a fixed rope, we set it to move faster. A fraction of a second and the rope above me clings to a stone like the palm, which rolls toward the rope beneath me. Peter went into the paddle for 2 minutes and I don't know if he got out, and the stone picked up speed. I yell "Rock!" With all the power left in the breath and I just hope to find Peter untouched and with the design of the helmet sponsors unaffected. As I enter the ridge, I notice that Marius is coming from above. I have no desire for stones so I jump back, and during the crossing I put my nails behind my neck and take care of the rocky corners.

Crossing is easy, but with 20kg on the back and dozens of sharp corners underneath, a slip is not an option. Even in the fixed rope that keeps us from the 1000m below.

Peter is ok, Marius comes too, but one of my carbon telescopic ski sticks falls on my backpack. It stops before a threshold, where a 10m jump follows. I don't want to leave it, it is expensive and I find it only in the States, so I get rid of the rope and start to descend, with both poles, the distance of 6m up to him. The corners grind on a plate hidden under the snow and I get stuck in the nuts. I still have 3m, but the hole below me looks like hell...

It's been a while since I had never made such an expedition. In the sense of long, hard and beyond hard. Coincidentally or not, the team is amazing. Peter and Marius seem to be taken from the job description. They climb like devils, chariots and finally I share a cigarette. And in the bad weather, there are days in a row under the basic conditions of the Spartan camp as if they were on holiday in Bali, enjoying a coffee, a pipe or a Romanian snack from Marius as if we were 5 stars, not in a tent tied with ropes so that the hurricane predicted the next day will not take us.

Opening a new route is not easy in Romania either. In the Himalayas even more so. And by far the biggest impediment is the unknown, estimating what awaits you the next day.

Despite the light way we try to take things, we are not here on leave. We did not come to have fun, we agreed to come and suffer. And I knew it would be hard.
But does the heavy have enough degrees of comparison? Because it is hard to describe in adjectives how things get harder, harder and harder and you start to wonder not if the hard time is longer, but how long it will increase in intensity and whether it will take over.

In the Himalayas, a normal route runs about 600-1000m a day for about 3000-4000m from the base camp to the summit. In other words, once acclimatized it reaches the peak in 3-4 days, plus a day-two for descent.

Here, for the 4000m, we also had good days when we advanced 200-300m. But there were 3 consecutive days, separated by a day's break, in which we worked 10-11 hours, we climbed almost 1000m to advance on the new route 20-30m. Then back down, not knowing when this work is going to end, rather than alpine.

In the section of the wall and the oven I saw about what material the strings, the pitons, the friends, Marius and Peter are made. Much louder than I would have thought.

I haven't beaten pythons for 10 years. I'm not happy to tell Peter that they are not well placed, consequently, with the thin dentist brand, they put two hands on the hammer tail. I'm Monica Seles, just don't scream. On overhanging portions, the fixed rope moves away from the horn and when climbing we need to pedal, in order to climb the rope with 2 blockers. No, not pedal bikers, but speleologists, anyway as mountaineering as a bunch of roses on the backpack. At a soft snow overhang, in which there are no nests, I come prepared with a shovel and after an hour, I advance 5m, removing the overhang and making steps from aluminum corners. I'm flea and broken. I freeze for an hour, with the fluff on me, along with Peter, while Marius skillfully passes the one-meter dash. Needless to say, after 4m he got off and took the climbing shoes, leaving his ankle boots with hangers on a python, freezing his legs terribly until the end of the rope length.

But at the end of the wall we do not expect a smooth ridge to the top, but a new technical section, lighter than the wall, but which requires fixed ropes. Another week's work.

After a month and a half of efforts, the most difficult part of the new route is opened. We are only at 5600m, we have not been able to acclimate ourselves above, but from here it is easier. You can walk on your feet, and even if it's not a walk in the park, you may go ahead and especially descend without fixed ropes.

We only need a window of good weather, with no wind above 30km / h in the peak area, for at least 4 days. I went slowly, being charged and not acclimatized.

This year, the window didn't come. It seems that, as in 2014, a disruption of the weather in the northern hemisphere leads to endless rains in Romania, floods in Slovakia and the lack of windows for oxygen-free ascents in the Himalayas.

We are in the tent at 5600m. Marius is left with his head in the hood of his sleeping bag, almost completely submerged. To the right, Peter began to rush. It's 5 o'clock, it lights up. The lucky guy in the middle starts putting ice in the aluminum pot and brews his coffee. With the blessings of the one who invented 3 + 1, we begin to indulge. Peter jokes, as every morning, about condensation. Marius looks calmly as if he were seeing the sea for the first time. I pull my head out of the tent and at 300m above us the windflags run along the whole ridge. 40-50 km/h wind.

Peter looked questioningly. I'm just nodding my head. I knew since we left that we would not have a good forecast, but we decided to try. Let's get to camp 2 and not come back unless we are in danger, because the wind does not let us. And possibly recover the equipment of several thousand euros from a hat. And the wind doesn't let us.

We have a long descent, heavy and risky in front, we can not detach yet. Not after a year of work, hopes and dreams. Not after 2 months of intense efforts, between the rocks and the ice. Slowly, there is an idea: We do not take everything! Let's leave what we can for next year. After all, the harder part is solved, we know the route to a great extent.

All we need is a decision for the year and a good weather window. I can hardly wait for the transition period to begin to train again.

Especially since I have both telescopic sticks.

And a valuable team: two forwarding colleagues I can count on, partners and sponsors who understand the Himalayan challenge because they are also used to such challenges in performance work.

I thank all those who were with me, with a thought of encouragement!

From The Himalayan Times (14 May 2019):

Mt Everest records season’s first summit as Sherpa climbers open summit route
Rajan Pokhrel

KATHMANDU: A team of experienced mountaineering guides successfully reached the top of the world opening a climbing route to the summit of Mt Everest this afternoon.
Gyanendra Shrestha, a liaison officer at the Everest base camp, told THT that at least eight Nepali climbers successfully fixed the climbing ropes from the South Col to the summit point becoming the season’s first team to scale Mt Everest at around 1:45 pm.

The team had reached the balcony area yesterday fixing ropes from the South Col, he added.

According to Iswari Paudel, Managing Director at Himalayan Guides, highly experienced rope-fixing high-altitude climbers who stood atop Mt Everest include Siddhi Bahadur Tamang from Dolakha, Pasdawa Sherpa from Sankhuwasabha, Dorji Gyaljen Sherpa and Pasang Dawa Sherpa from Solukhumbu, Ang Phurba Sherpa, Dawa Nurbu Sherpa, Ming Dorchi Sherpa and Sangbu Bhote from Sankhuwasabha.

Tamang-led rope fixing team struggled for hours to reach the summit by placing ropes above the South Col, Shrestha added.

The Expedition Operators Association of Nepal has assigned Himalayan Guides to fix the route above Camp II this season while a team of icefall doctors assigned by the Sagarmatha Pollution Control Committee opened the climbing route from the base camp to Camp II.

OTHERSMTS: Climbed Lobuche on 20 April; summited Pumori (EVER-191-02)
ROUTEMEMO:
Summit team left at 7:30 pm from C4, very windy cold. 5 set off together (Hollis, Magitsis, Akkad, Anastasopoulou, Devi, then Dawa Steven + 2 Chinese followed (3rd Chinese returned from C3 or C4). Knee-high snow from the Balcony, difficult to walk and break the trail, so Pemba Tshering and Hollis broke the trail. Akkad had mask problems with his beard (Akkad's Sherpa was Samden Bhote; everyone said he was the best out of the whole team).

Further details of climb from Hollis: On 20/05 started at 8 pm from C4; it was quite windy. Probably 20 climbers ahead. Nick and Pemba Tshering passed them in about 1.5 hrs, breaking the trail. Snowed-over trail, powdery, was hard; had to pull out ropes up over the snow. On the Balcony did an O2 change and waited for other teams to help break trail. Upon seeing others weren't in good shape to do so, Nick and Pemba pushed on ahead themselves. Dropped down from South summit and did an O2 change. Nick was waiting for the famous Hillary Step section. Getting up to the South Summit with the steep rock section turned out to be more difficult than the Hillary Step, which he ended up not even noticing on the way up, it was so insignificant, and suddenly they were at the summit. This is Nick's first attempt. He had some mask issues, ice blocked it, a bit frightening.

Hollis reached summit on 21/05 at 4:55 am just as it was getting light. His vision was cloudy so descended right away. Spent less than 10 mins on summit.

Akkad arrived at 5:24 am, Anastasopoulou just behind a couple of seconds. 15 minutes on summit.

Devi with Sherpa summited at 5:58 am, stayed on the summit for 20 minutes. They had started from C4 at 8:30 pm. Back to C4 at 9 am. Very windy and cold on summit day, not good weather. 

Magitsis abandoned climbed at 4:30 am at South Summit due to frozen air valve on mask (not able to breathe). Tshering Tashi descended ith him.

Hollis got to C4 with vision problems. Lay down for 2.5 hrs. Then eyes got a little better and pushed down (still 21/05) and made it to C2 by 5 pm. 22/05 left C2 at 8am descended to BC.

Akkad arrived back at C4 at 11:30 am, took one hour rest, then 12:30 pm went to C2 arriving at 9:30 pm. Stayed one night in C2 and 22/05 to BC.

21/05 Magitsis arrived back at C4 at 7 am and Anastasopoulou at around 11 am. Then they both went straight down to C2 alone without Sherpas. They had problems with Sherpas who didn't follow them. Slept on the rocks in down suits in tents and later at around 9 pm both their Sherpas arrived with sleeping bags, etc. 22/05 everyone went to BC. 

On 23/05 team heli to Pheriche, to Lukla then to KTM.

Other details of the rest of the team provided by Hollis:

Magitsis from Greece used O2 from C3; he did not summit. Clay (Indian from UK) arrived late to BC, went on rotation to C2 and heli-vaced out back to Ktm way before summit push. Randolph (from USA) also came in late and got altitude sickness at Lobuche, so he was evacuated early.

27/05 summit team left C4 at 9 pm. Straightforward climb, no winds, perfect day. They spent about 15 minutes on the summit and descended to C4 where they arrived at 10 am. Grom and Mrika and Arianit Nikci descended to C2.

Lhotse Smt 23/05 by Kitri and Namgyal Sherpa at 8:30 am; Freire, Badar and Kami Sherpa at 6 am.

After summiting Everest on 22/05 at 2:30 am, Lhotse summit team descended to Everest C4, where they arrived at 8:30 am. They stayed at Everest C4 and rested there. At 12:30 am they traversed from Everest C4 to Lhotse C4, where they had a short 10-minute break. They continued to the summit, stayed there for about 10 minutes and descended to C2, where they arrived at 4 pm. On 24/05 down to BC.

Kulish's death: Kulish descended in 3 hours and was strong and in good spirits on the way down. He was fine for about four hours at C4. At 4 pm, Ceppi the guide went to check on Kulish in his tent and found him dead with his oxygen mask on. He did CPR for about 25 minutes with Prakash but no success. The body (packaged up) is still in the tent at the South col. On 28/05 Montesinos and Ceppi descended to C2 while the others descended to BC.

On 29/05, everyone down at BC.

Missirlian reached C1 early in rotation. He "could not find his why" and went home.

Beaudoin did not climb on Everest at all. He left due to family issues.

Tepekian reached C1 early in the rotation. He had a bad cough, tore some cartilage and left the expedition early.

Gregory reached C2 26/04. He had severe AMS and left the expedition.

Kalb (supposed to climb Lhotse) reached C2 and had AMS symptoms there and left the expedition early.

Stewart reached C1 early in the trip. He had chest pain and AMS symptoms and left expedition.

Oxygen stolen: 73 bottles of oxygen went missing on the 22 May along with tents. Hamill held team at C2, used their redundancy bottles and also had some help from Adventure Consultants with 14 bottles and Alpine Ascents. All of Hamill's 2nd team summited with no oxygen dramas at all and were on full flows so they managed to pull it together. 

Team left BC on 29/05.

Summit team left C4 at 8:45 pm on 15/05. It was reasonably quiet on the route, calm to start, but windy a bit later. Straightforward climb. They spent about 15 minutes on the summit. Just below the Hillary Step, Khumalo told Hanna that she was feeling weak and suddenly collapsed and rolled her eyes. Hanna took her Sherpa's oxygen, put it on Khumalo and she came to again. They found that her oxygen mask was frozen, which must have been the problem. They cleaned the mask and the team descended to the Balcony together. They changed oxygen bottles and as Lawless was feeling a bit altitude sick, but was still faster, he continued dowh with Tempa Bhote ahead of the group.

Noel Hanna's account:

Hanna was behind the girls coming down to C4, where they arrived at about 4 pm (Lawless must have arrived an hour earlier). When Hanna went to the tent he was sharing with Lawless on the way up, but it was occupied by someone else, who then left. Hanna thought that Lawless had gone to another tent. But then Temba came and told Hanna that Lawless had been missing after having gone to the toilet. Hanna told Copeland who like Lawless had an inReach. Lawless had must have sent a message to his wife to tell her to get in touch with Copeland, which she did. As the wife had forwarded the message, Hanna had the coordinates to where he was. It was about 5 pm and the weather was getting worse, but they still followed the GPS coordinates and could not find him.

On 17/05 the team descended to C2, a Sherpa had found Lawless's rucksack. The harness was in the rucksack. On 18/05 down to BC.

The family wanted Hanna to find the body (the Irish government guaranteed SST that the money would be paid for the search). On 22/05 they started the search in the evening on the bottom of the Lhotse face as they had found the rucksack there. Checked all the crevasses and could not find anything. At 11 pm on 22/05, Hanna and seven Sherpas ascended to C4 and searched for the body at C4, where they arrived at 7 am in the morning (Hanna a bit later). They started the search immediately. They found broken crampons and a broken oxygen mask. They searched until about 1 pm and descended to C2, where they arrived at C2. The traffic coming down was a nightmare. On 24/05 Hanna was picked up by heli at C2 and they flew to about 7200m and looked, but could not find him. They gave up at about 5 pm and flew to BC.

Saray Khumalo’s account:

The rest of the group arrived at C4 in the early afternoon. When they reached there they noticed that Lawless was not there. Temba, his Sherpa, said that he had gone to the toilet at C4 away. Temba must have gone into his tent when Lawless went to the toilet and did not notice that he did not come back. At the time of the interview, Hanna was still on the mountain looking for the body.

On 17/05 they descended to C2; on 18/05 BC. The whole team stayed at BC until 22/05 to support Noel in the search and recovery of Lawless; however, so far they still have not found his body.

Ali Baig and Pemba Sherpa left C4 at 6:30 pm on 21/05. Milind Raskhar, Don Cash, Khai from the US (Vietnam) and the Indian Brothers (Mahendra and Hitendra Mahajan) also left around the same time.

At the beginning Ali Baig and Pemba had a big queue in front of them until the Balcony, however, they overtook most of them. From the Hillary Step they were the first people. It was very cold and windy. They stayed about 10 to 15 minutes on the summit (Lakpa Tendu Sherpa and Dawa Sherpa from Survey team, Chhiring Jambu from a different team arrived shortly after them) and they descended to C4, where they arrived at 6:30 am. On the way down, they had to wait for the crowds to come up for nearly three hours. Two hours at the Hillary Step only. 

When they reached the South Summit, Ali Baig noticed that his eyes were hurting and he could not see properly. He had probably wind burn as he was not wearing glasses on the ascent. The changed oxygen at the South Summit and rested a bit. At the rock section below the South Summit they were held up again for about half an hour.

At C4 they rested for a short time. Ali Baig put black tea bags on his eyes and then they continued to C4 with very bad eyesight. They arrived at C2 at 2 pm. Ali Baig’s eyes got worse and they organised a helicopter rescue from C2. They were picked up by heli on 23/05 at 8 am. He went to ER at BC and was then transferred to Kathmandu to CIWEC, where he spent one night. His eyes are Ok now even though he still cannot face sunlight.
